<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Q1. Distinguish between *Economic Growth* and *Economic Development*.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Economic Growth** is a *quantitative* increase in a country's real output of goods and services over a period, typically measured by the rise in real GDP or GNP per capita. It is a *narrower* concept, focusing on the *size* of the economy.
- **Economic Development** is a *qualitative and broader* concept that includes economic growth plus improvements in the *living standard* of people, reduction in poverty and inequality, better health and education, and structural changes in the economy.
- In short: **Growth ⊂ Development**. A country can have growth without development (e.g., oil-rich nations with poor literacy), but development cannot occur without sustained growth.
*(Examiner's note: A neat comparison table fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. What is the Human Development Index (HDI)? List its three component dimensions.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- The **Human Development Index (HDI)**, introduced by the **United Nations Development Programme (UNDP)** in 1990 (conceived by **Mahbub ul Haq** with theoretical support from **Amartya Sen**), is a composite statistic used to measure a country's overall achievement in key dimensions of human development.
- It is the **geometric mean** of three normalized indices, each ranging from 0 to 1:
  1. **Health Dimension** — measured by *Life Expectancy at Birth* (normalized between 20 and 85 years).
  2. **Education Dimension** — measured by the *Mean Years of Schooling* (of adults aged 25+) and *Expected Years of Schooling* (for children entering school).
  3. **Standard of Living / Income Dimension** — measured by *GNI per capita at Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)*, in natural log scale (capped at \$75,000).
- HDI ranges from **0 (lowest)** to **1 (highest)**, and is published annually in the UNDP *Human Development Report*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Discuss the major theories of development. Compare the Modernization Theory and the Dependency Theory.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Development theories attempt to explain *how* and *why* societies progress. Two of the most influential — and opposing — frameworks are Modernization Theory and Dependency Theory.

**Modernization Theory (3 marks):**
- Propounded by **W.W. Rostow** in *The Stages of Economic Growth* (1960).
- All societies evolve through **five stages**:
  1. **Traditional Society** — agriculture-based, low productivity.
  2. **Pre-conditions for Take-off** — emergence of elite-led reforms.
  3. **Take-off** — industrial revolution, sustained growth.
  4. **Drive to Maturity** — diversification of the economy.
  5. **Age of High Mass Consumption** — consumer-driven services.
- Assumes that the Western/European path is the *universal model* of progress.
- Critics: Eurocentric, ignores colonial exploitation, ignores cultural diversity.

**Dependency Theory (2 marks):**
- Developed by **Raúl Prebisch** (ECLAC) and **Andre Gunder Frank** in the 1960s–70s.
- Argues that *underdevelopment* of the Global South is **not** a "primitive" condition but the **direct result** of historical and ongoing economic exploitation by the Global North.
- Core concept: the **"Development of Underdevelopment"** — wealth flows from periphery to core, impoverishing the periphery.
- Policy prescription: **delinking**, self-reliance, South–South cooperation.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Modernization | Dependency |
|---|---|---|
| View of Global South | Backward but capable of growth | Exploited and structurally constrained |
| Role of North | Model to emulate | Exploiter to resist |
| Solution | Aid, investment, technology transfer | Self-reliance, structural change |

**Conclusion:** Both theories offer valuable lenses, but neither alone is sufficient. Post-1990s frameworks (Capability Approach, Sustainable Development) integrate insights from both.

---

**(b) Explain the three pillars of Sustainable Development. How do they relate to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**The Three Pillars (3 marks):**
1. **Economic Pillar** — focuses on *prosperity* — sustainable growth, decent work, innovation, and equitable distribution. Linked to SDGs 1, 2, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12.
2. **Social Pillar** — focuses on *people* — health, education, gender equity, inclusion, social justice. Linked to SDGs 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11.
3. **Environmental Pillar** — focuses on *planet* — climate action, biodiversity, resource conservation. Linked to SDGs 6, 13, 14, 15.

> **Plus the often-overlooked 4th and 5th pillars:**
4. **Peace (SDG 16)** — just and inclusive societies.
5. **Partnership (SDG 17)** — global cooperation for implementation.

**Relation to SDGs (3 marks):**
- The SDGs (2015–2030, **17 goals, 169 targets, 231 indicators**) operationalize the three pillars into measurable global targets.
- Goal 17 (Partnership) acknowledges that *no single country* can achieve these alone.
- The **"5 Ps" framework** — People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership — is a direct mapping of the pillars onto the SDG architecture.
- The SDGs are *integrated and indivisible*; e.g., Goal 13 (Climate Action) affects Goal 2 (Zero Hunger) and Goal 3 (Good Health).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The three pillars, when translated into SDGs, provide a *shared global roadmap* for balancing human well-being with planetary health — a central concern of 21st-century engineering ethics.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a) Define Human Development. Explain the Human Development Index (HDI) with its formula. Critically evaluate HDI as a measure of development.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
- **Human Development**, as defined by the **UNDP**, is the process of *enlarging people's choices* — to lead a long and healthy life, to acquire knowledge, and to have access to resources needed for a decent standard of living. (Mahbub ul Haq).
- Amartya Sen's **Capability Approach** underpins it: development is the expansion of *real freedoms* (capabilities) people have to live the life they value.

**HDI Formula (2.5 marks):**
- HDI is the **geometric mean** of three normalized indices:

$$\text{HDI} = \sqrt[3]{I_{\text{Health}} \times I_{\text{Education}} \times I_{\text{Income}}}$$

where

$$I_{\text{Health}} = \dfrac{\text{LE} - 20}{85 - 20}, \quad I_{\text{Education}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MYS}}{15} + \dfrac{\text{EYS}}{18}\right), \quad I_{\text{Income}} = \dfrac{\ln(\text{GNI}_{ppc}) - \ln(100)}{\ln(75{,}000) - \ln(100)}$$

- Each index ranges 0–1. The geometric mean penalizes *imbalance* — a country with very high income but low education will have a lower HDI.

**Critical Evaluation (3 marks):**
- **Strengths:**
  1. Captures *non-economic* dimensions of well-being.
  2. Easy to compute and compare across countries.
  3. Politically influential — shifts debate from GDP to people.
- **Limitations:**
  1. **Ignores inequality** — HDI of a country with 1 billionaire and 1 billion poor can be the same as an egalitarian country. (GII and IHDI correct this.)
  2. **Ignores environment** — does not penalize ecological destruction. (EPI complements it.)
  3. **Ignores political freedoms and human rights** — purely outcome-based.
  4. **Aggregation hides regional disparities** within a country.
  5. **Cap on GNI (\$75,000)** may understate very high development.

**Conclusion (≈ half mark):** HDI is a *powerful but incomplete* compass. Modern development assessment uses a *family* of indices — HDI, IHDI, GII, GHI, EPI — for a holistic view.

---

**(b) Write a detailed note on the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Why did the world transition from MDGs to SDGs?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**MDGs (2000–2015) — 2 marks:**
- 8 goals, framed at the UN Millennium Summit (2000).
- Focus: poverty, education, gender, health, environment.
- Major achievement: **extreme poverty halved globally**; child mortality reduced.
- Weaknesses: donor-driven, narrow, ignored climate and inequality.

**SDGs (2015–2030) — 2 marks:**
- 17 goals, 169 targets, 231 indicators.
- Universal — apply to *all* countries, not just developing ones.
- Built on **5 Ps**: People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership.
- Emphasize **integration, equity, and "Leave No One Behind"**.

**Reasons for Transition (2 marks):**
1. **Universality** — MDGs ignored challenges of middle- and high-income nations.
2. **Climate crisis** — MDG 7 was weak; SDG 13 (Climate Action) and 14, 15 strengthen it.
3. **Inequality** — MDGs aggregated progress; SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and GII mainstream equity.
4. **Peace & Governance** — MDGs omitted; SDG 16 added.
5. **Partnership** — MDG 8 was weak; SDG 17 (Partnerships) is now central.
6. **Interlinkages** — SDGs explicitly recognize that goals are *interdependent*.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The MDG→SDG transition reflects the global recognition that **development is a multidimensional, integrated, and universal agenda** — and that no nation can achieve it in isolation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in "Development" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Growth" with "Development"** — Growth is *quantitative* (GDP rise); Development is *qualitative* (well-being, equity). Writing one when the other is asked = -2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Brundtland definition** — the *exact* phrase "development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs" is a guaranteed scoring line for 1 mark. Paraphrase it wrongly and lose it.
> 3. **Mixing up MDG/SDG counts** — MDGs = **8**, SDGs = **17**. Mixing them is the most common factual error.
> 4. **Treating HDI as a pure economic index** — it is a *composite* of health, education, and income. Missing any one dimension = -1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards critical thinking. Always include a concluding paragraph with the **engineering-ethics angle** (e.g., "Engineers must design with the 3 pillars in mind").
> 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — A 14-mark answer without a single labelled diagram/flowchart/matrix forfeits 1–2 marks under the KTU 2024 scheme's "presentation" sub-criterion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Development ≠ Growth.** Growth = quantitative (GDP); Development = growth + equity + well-being.
- [ ] **Sustainable Development** = *Brundtland definition* (1987). Three pillars: **Economic, Social, Environmental** (+ Peace, Partnership in SDGs).
- [ ] **HDI** = $\sqrt[3]{I_{\text{Health}} \cdot I_{\text{Education}} \cdot I_{\text{Income}}}$. Geometric mean. Range 0–1.
- [ ] HDI normalization: LE 20–85, MYS 0–15, EYS 0–18, GNI \$100–\$75,000 (log scale).
- [ ] **MDGs = 8** (2000–2015), **SDGs = 17** (2015–2030). SDGs are universal and integrated.
- [ ] **5 Ps of SDGs**: People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership.
- [ ] **Capability Approach** → Amartya Sen. **HDI** → Mahbub ul Haq / UNDP (1990).
- [ ] **Rostow's 5 Stages** of Modernization. **Prebisch/Frank** → Dependency Theory.
- [ ] **Key indicators**: GDP, GNI, HDI, GII, GHI, EPI, Gini, Ecological Footprint.
- [ ] **Sustainable Development Goal 17** is the implementation/partnership goal — often missed by students.
- [ ] **Engineering-ethics link**: As future engineers, you must evaluate projects against the 3 pillars, not just profitability.
- [ ] Always end a 14-mark answer with a **conclusion + engineering-ethics reflection** — this is a KTU 2024 valuation differentiator.

---

> **End of Module 1 – Topic: Development Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) syllabus for UCHUT347 – Engineering Ethics and Sustainable Development.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Research Domains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** and Research Domains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Research

> [!NOTE]
> **Research (KTU 2024 Definition):** Research is a systematic, methodical, and rigorous process of inquiry that aims to discover, interpret, or revise facts, theories, applications, or new uses of existing knowledge, employing disciplined scientific methodologies to generate new, validated, and reproducible insights.

The **Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR)** and **University Grants Commission (UGC)** define research as "any systematic activity designed to contribute to the advancement of knowledge in a particular field."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of UCHUT347, research is not merely an academic exercise — it is the foundational instrument through which engineering ethics is investigated, validated, and applied. The 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum emphasizes **responsible research conduct** as a core competency of the modern engineer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cartographer's Journey

Imagine you are a **cartographer** tasked with mapping an uncharted island:

- **Before research:** You stand at the shoreline of a dense jungle (an unknown problem).
- **During research:** You cut trails (methodology), take compass readings (data collection), mark landmarks (analysis), and cross-check with star positions (validation).
- **After research:** You produce a new, more accurate map (peer-reviewed knowledge) that others can trust and build upon.

> The "island" in engineering research may be a new **material**, **algorithm**, **sustainable process**, or **ethical framework**. Without disciplined mapping (research), innovation becomes guesswork.

### 1.3 Research Domain — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Research Domain:** A research domain is a defined, specialized area of inquiry characterized by a coherent set of problems, methodologies, theoretical frameworks, and community of practitioners. In engineering, domains are typically bounded by discipline (e.g., Civil, Computer Science), application (e.g., healthcare, energy), or methodology (e.g., experimental, computational).

### 1.4 The Six Pillars of Research (KTU Conceptual Framework)

| Pillar | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Originality** | Contribution must be novel — not previously published or patented. |
| **Clarity** | Research questions, hypotheses, and methods must be unambiguous. |
| **Reproducibility** | Independent researchers must be able to replicate results. |
| **Validity** | Internal consistency and external generalizability. |
| **Ethical Compliance** | Adherence to IRB, CPCSEA, IAEC, or relevant ethical guidelines. |
| **Impact** | Contribution to knowledge, society, environment, or economy. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When answering KTU questions on research, always reference these six pillars. Examiners reward answers that demonstrate awareness of **methodology + ethics + impact** as a unified construct.

### 1.5 Why Research Domains Matter in Engineering Ethics

Engineering ethics without research is **opinion**. Research without ethics is **danger**. The convergence of both — what the KTU 2024 scheme calls **"Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI)"** — is the cornerstone of modern sustainable engineering practice.

> **Key Insight:** The 2024 NEP framework explicitly mandates that every engineering graduate must demonstrate the ability to *identify research gaps*, *apply appropriate methodology*, and *evaluate ethical implications* of research outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Research

Research can be classified along **four primary axes** recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### Axis 1: By Purpose
- **Basic Research (Pure/Fundamental):** Driven by curiosity; seeks to expand fundamental knowledge without immediate practical application.
  - *Example:* Investigating the mathematical properties of novel graphene lattices.
- **Applied Research:** Directed toward solving a specific, practical problem.
  - *Example:* Designing a low-cost water filter for Kerala flood-affected regions.
- **Experimental Research:** Systematically manipulates variables to establish cause-effect relationships.
  - *Example:* Testing concrete strength with varying fly-ash content.
- **Descriptive Research:** Documents characteristics of a phenomenon without manipulating variables.
  - *Example:* Surveying post-disaster housing conditions in Wayanad.

#### Axis 2: By Approach
- **Quantitative Research:** Numerical data, statistical analysis, measurable outcomes.
- **Qualitative Research:** Thematic analysis, interviews, ethnographic observation.
- **Mixed-Methods Research:** Combines both for holistic understanding.

#### Axis 3: By Inference
- **Deductive Research:** Theory → Hypothesis → Test → Confirmation.
- **Inductive Research:** Observation → Pattern → Theory.
- **Abductive Research:** Observation → Best Explanation → Hypothesis.

#### Axis 4: By Time Dimension
- **Cross-Sectional:** Data collected at a single point in time.
- **Longitudinal:** Data collected over extended periods.
- **Retrospective:** Looking back at historical data.
- **Prospective:** Forward-looking, tracking new cases.

### 2.2 Research Domains in Engineering (KTU 2024 Mapping)

The following matrix is **directly aligned with the UCHUT347 syllabus outcomes** and maps each research domain to its ethical considerations:

| Engineering Research Domain | Core Focus | Primary Ethical Concern | Sustainability Link |
|------------------------------|------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Materials Science** | New composites, nanomaterials | Toxicity, end-of-life disposal | Circular materials, biodegradables |
| **Energy Systems** | Renewable, storage, grid | Resource extraction, e-waste | UN SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy) |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Implants, prosthetics, AI diagnostics | Patient consent, data privacy | SDG 3 (Good Health & Well-being) |
| **Artificial Intelligence / ML** | Algorithms, autonomous systems | Bias, accountability, transparency | SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure) |
| **Civil & Structural** | Smart cities, resilient infra | Public safety, displacement | SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities) |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Pollution control, remediation | Environmental justice | SDG 6, 13, 14, 15 |
| **Robotics & Automation** | Human-robot interaction, exoskeletons | Job displacement, autonomy | SDG 8 (Decent Work) |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | UAVs, propulsion | Dual-use, weaponization | SDG 16 (Peace, Justice) |
| **Agricultural Engineering** | Precision farming, GMOs | Biodiversity, farmer rights | SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability Imperative:** Every research domain in the 21st century must address the **triple bottom line** — *People, Planet, Profit* (Elkington, 1994). KTU examiners frequently frame questions around this paradigm.

### 2.3 The Research Process — Phased Framework

Engineering research, as endorsed by KTU and AICTE, follows a **six-phase cycle**:

1. **Phase 1 — Problem Identification:** Recognize a gap, need, or contradiction in existing knowledge.
2. **Phase 2 — Literature Review:** Systematic mapping of prior work using tools like **Scopus**, **Web of Science**, **Google Scholar**.
3. **Phase 3 — Hypothesis Formulation:** A testable, falsifiable statement.
4. **Phase 4 — Methodology Design:** Selection of tools, models, simulations, experiments.
5. **Phase 5 — Data Collection & Analysis:** Rigorous execution with ethical safeguards.
6. **Phase 6 — Dissemination & Peer Review:** Publication, patent filing, conference presentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Each phase has an embedded ethical checkpoint. Skipping ethical review at any phase invalidates the entire research, regardless of scientific merit.

### 2.4 Research Domains & Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Mapping

The **17 UN SDGs** provide a globally recognized framework for categorizing research impact:

| SDG | Research Domain Examples |
|-----|--------------------------|
| SDG 1 — No Poverty | Microfinance engineering, affordable housing |
| SDG 4 — Quality Education | EdTech, open educational resources |
| SDG 5 — Gender Equality | Inclusive design, women in STEM studies |
| SDG 7 — Clean Energy | Solar, wind, hydrogen fuel cells |
| SDG 9 — Industry/Innovation | Smart manufacturing, Industry 4.0 |
| SDG 12 — Responsible Consumption | Green chemistry, waste valorization |
| SDG 13 — Climate Action | Carbon capture, climate modeling |
| SDG 17 — Partnerships | International research collaborations |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Research Domains

- **Policy Making:** Government committees rely on domain-specific research (e.g., **IPCC** climate reports).
- **Industrial Innovation:** Companies allocate **6–10% of revenue** to R&D in high-tech sectors.
- **Public Health:** Biomedical research domains have saved millions of lives through vaccine development.
- **Environmental Protection:** Domain research in environmental engineering has led to the **Clean Ganga Mission**, **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation** (AMRUT), and Kerala's **Suchitwa Mission**.

> **Industrial Insight:** India's R&D expenditure has risen from **0.6% of GDP (2014)** to approximately **0.65–0.7% (2024)**, with a national target of **2% by 2030** (NEP 2020). This signals growing demand for ethically-grounded, domain-aware researchers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Framework — Choosing a Research Domain

Since this is a conceptual (non-mathematical) topic, the "derivation" here is the **decision logic** that a researcher follows to select and justify a research domain. We present this as an exhaustive, step-by-step analytical matrix.

---

#### Step 1: Self-Assessment & Capacity Mapping

The researcher must first evaluate:

$$
\text{Research Readiness Index (RRI)} = \frac{(K + M + E + T + F)}{5}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = Domain Knowledge (scale 0–10)
- $M$ = Methodological Competence (scale 0–10)
- $E$ = Ethical Awareness (scale 0–10)
- $T$ = Time Availability in semesters (scale 0–10)
- $F$ = Funding/Resource Availability (scale 0–10)

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{RRI} \geq 8$: Ready for advanced, original research.
- $\text{RRI} \in [5, 8]$: Suitable for applied/descriptive research.
- $\text{RRI} < 5$: Recommend preliminary literature review or systematic review.

---

#### Step 2: Domain Identification Matrix

The student systematically scores candidate domains against six criteria:

$$
\text{Domain Suitability Score (DSS)} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where each criterion $s_i$ is scored 1–10 and $w_i$ is the importance weight (summing to 1.0):

| Criterion $i$ | Weight $w_i$ (Typical) | Description |
|---------------|------------------------|-------------|
| Personal Interest | 0.20 | Long-term motivation |
| Societal Need | 0.20 | Relevance to local/regional issues |
| SDG Alignment | 0.15 | UN SDG contribution potential |
| Resource Feasibility | 0.15 | Lab, data, mentorship available |
| Career Trajectory | 0.15 | Industry demand, employability |
| Ethical Clarity | 0.15 | Absence of major ethical dilemmas |

**Example Calculation:**

Suppose a student is choosing between *Renewable Energy Systems* and *Social Media Algorithm Analysis*:

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Renewable Energy $s_i$ | Social Media $s_i$ |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Personal Interest | 0.20 | 9 | 6 |
| Societal Need | 0.20 | 9 | 5 |
| SDG Alignment | 0.15 | 10 | 4 |
| Resource Feasibility | 0.15 | 7 | 8 |
| Career Trajectory | 0.15 | 9 | 9 |
| Ethical Clarity | 0.15 | 8 | 5 |

**Computation for Renewable Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DSS}_{\text{Renewable}} &= (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 10) \\
&\quad + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.80 + 1.50 + 1.05 + 1.35 + 1.20 \\
&= 8.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Social Media Algorithms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DSS}_{\text{Social}} &= (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
&\quad + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 5) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.75 \\
&= 6.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Select Domain} &= \arg\max_{d \in D} \text{DSS}(d) \\
&= \text{Renewable Energy Systems (since } 8.70 > 6.10\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 3: Ethical Screening — The KTU RRI Checklist

Every domain candidate must pass through the **Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI)** screening:

| Checkpoint | Question | Pass/Fail |
|------------|----------|-----------|
| **Dual-Use Risk** | Can the research outcomes be misused for harm? | |
| **Vulnerable Population** | Does the research involve children, animals, or marginalized groups? | |
| **Environmental Impact** | Will the research generate hazardous waste or emissions? | |
| **Data Privacy** | Does the research involve personal/sensitive data? | |
| **Informed Consent** | Are participants fully informed of risks? | |
| **Conflict of Interest** | Are funding sources creating bias? | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure on any checkpoint = Mandatory review by Institutional Ethics Committee (IEC) before proceeding.**

---

#### Step 4: Research Question Formulation (FINER + SMART)

The research question must satisfy the **FINER criteria** (Hulley et al.) and **SMART attributes**:

$$
\text{Quality Research Question} = \text{F} \cap \text{I} \cap \text{N} \cap \text{E} \cap \text{R} \cap \text{S} \cap \text{M} \cap \text{A} \cap \text{R} \cap \text{T}
$$

Where:
- **F** = Feasible
- **I** = Interesting
- **N** = Novel
- **E** = Ethical
- **R** = Relevant
- **S** = Specific
- **M** = Measurable
- **A** = Achievable
- **R** = Realistic
- **T** = Time-bound

---

#### Step 5: Stakeholder Mapping & Impact Assessment

The researcher identifies all stakeholders and projects impact:

$$
\text{Total Impact} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_j \cdot P_j
$$

Where $I_j$ = impact magnitude (–10 to +10) and $P_j$ = probability of occurrence (0 to 1).

**Rule:** If Total Impact $< 0$, the research is ethically questionable regardless of scientific merit.

---

#### Step 6: Dissemination Strategy Decision Tree

```
Outcomes of Research
       │
       ├── Positive + Reproducible → Publish in Peer-Reviewed Journal
       ├── Positive + Novel + Applicable → File Patent (India/IPR Cell)
       ├── Negative or Null Result → Publish (prevents duplication)
       ├── Sensitive/Dual-Use → Classified, Restricted Access
       └── Unethical Discovery → Suppress, Notify IEC
```

### 3.2 Complete Decision Flowchart (Textual Representation)

1. **Identify** societal/technical problem
2. **Conduct** systematic literature review (SLR)
3. **Score** candidate domains using DSS formula
4. **Screen** selected domain using RRI checklist
5. **Formulate** SMART/FINER research question
6. **Design** methodology with ethical safeguards
7. **Execute** pilot study to validate tools
8. **Collect** data under informed consent
9. **Analyze** using appropriate statistical/qualitative tools
10. **Validate** through triangulation & peer review
11. **Document** every phase for reproducibility
12. **Disseminate** via journal, conference, patent, or open-access repository
13. **Reflect** on ethical implications and societal impact
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics


### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Research Domain Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Domain Selection] --> Q1{Is there a clear societal problem?}
    Q1 -- No --> STOP1[Stop and Reformulate]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is domain knowledge adequate?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Preliminary Reading and Mentorship]
    Q3 --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Are resources available?}
    Q4 -- No --> Q5[Seek Funding DST SERB KTU]

    Q10 --> Q1
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q11[Formulate FINER SMART Question]

```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Six Pillars of Research Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RQ[Research Quality] --> P1[Originality]

    P5 --> INST[Institutional Ethics Committee]

    P6 --> LOC[Local Community Benefit]

    P1 --> NOV[Novelty Check Turnitin]
```
