# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> *Define the term "Water Scarcity" and briefly explain the Falkenmark Indicator.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1 mark):** Water scarcity is a condition in which the per-capita annual renewable freshwater supply falls below the level required to meet domestic, agricultural, industrial, and ecological needs.

**Falkenmark Indicator (2 marks):** Proposed by Malin Falkenmark in 1989, it is a quantitative measure that expresses water availability as per-capita renewable freshwater in m³/person/year. The thresholds are:
- **> 1,700 m³/person/year** → No water stress
- **1,000 – 1,700 m³/person/year** → Water stressed
- **500 – 1,000 m³/person/year** → Water scarce
- **< 500 m³/person/year** → Absolute scarcity

The indicator is widely used because of its **simplicity, data availability, and policy clarity**.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> *List any three major categories of water pollutants with one example each.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
1. **Biological Pollutants (1 mark):** *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae* — sources include domestic sewage and animal waste; cause waterborne diseases.
2. **Chemical Pollutants – Inorganic (1 mark):** Heavy metals like lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd) — sources include mining, electroplating, batteries; cause neurological and kidney damage.
3. **Chemical Pollutants – Organic (1 mark):** Pesticides (DDT, organochlorines), hydrocarbons — sources include agriculture and oil spills; cause cancer, endocrine disruption, and biomagnification in food chains.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Explain the various **causes of water scarcity** with reference to India. Discuss the role of climate change in exacerbating scarcity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **major sources and categories of water pollution**. What is the role of **BOD and DO** in assessing river water quality? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Causes of Water Scarcity in India (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Water scarcity in India is a **multidimensional crisis** arising from the convergence of demographic, economic, climatic, and infrastructural factors. With per-capita water availability at **≈ 1,367 m³/person/year** (2025), India is already in the water-stressed zone.

**1. Population Pressure (1 mark):** India is the world's most populous country with **1.45 billion people** (2025). Domestic water demand in urban India is **135–200 litres/person/day**, multiplying stress on municipal supply systems.

**2. Agricultural Overconsumption (1 mark):** Agriculture consumes **≈ 80%** of India's freshwater. Water-intensive crops (sugarcane, paddy) and inefficient flood irrigation dominate. **Drip irrigation covers only 5%** of irrigated area as against Israel's **> 90%**.

**3. Industrialization (1 mark):** Thermal power plants, textiles, tanneries, and steel industries consume and pollute enormous volumes. Thermal plants alone use **≈ 22 billion m³/year**.

**4. Urbanization and Lifestyle (1 mark):** Encroachment of wetlands, concretization reducing groundwater recharge, and the cultural preference for potable water in flush toilets (where recycled water could suffice) escalate urban water footprints.

**5. Climate Change (1 mark):** Erratic monsoons, **Himalayan glacier retreat** (65% loss projected by 2100 under RCP 8.5), rising sea levels causing **saline intrusion in Kerala's coastal aquifers**, and increased evapotranspiration (2–3% per decade) reduce renewable supply.

**6. Inadequate Infrastructure and Governance (1 mark):** Leaking urban networks lose **30–40%** of supplied water; inter-state river disputes (Cauvery, Krishna) prevent equitable allocation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Water scarcity in India is therefore a **wicked problem** requiring a portfolio of engineering (recycling, drip irrigation, desalination), policy (Jal Jeevan Mission, Atal Mission for Rejuvenation), and ethical (water as a human right) interventions.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Sources, Categories, and BOD/DO Assessment of Water Pollution (7 Marks)

**Major Sources of Water Pollution (3 marks):**

| Source | Example | Pollutant Type |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| Domestic sewage | Untreated black water from cities | Biological, organic |
| Industrial effluent | Tanneries (Cr), textile dyes, distilleries | Heavy metals, organic |
| Agricultural runoff | Fertilizers, pesticides | Nitrates, organochlorines |
| Oil spills | Maritime accidents | Hydrocarbons |
| Atmospheric | Acid rain (SO₂, NOₓ) | Acidic deposition |
| Emerging | Plastics, pharmaceuticals | Microplastics, PFAS |

**Categories (2 marks):** Water pollutants are classified as **biological, chemical (organic/inorganic), physical, radiological, and emerging**. Each category has distinct removal mechanisms in treatment plants — biological pollutants are removed by **activated sludge**, chemical pollutants by **chemical precipitation and adsorption**, and emerging pollutants by **advanced oxidation and reverse osmosis**.

**BOD and DO in River Water Quality (2 marks):**
- **BOD** measures the oxygen required by microorganisms to decompose organic matter. A high BOD indicates heavy organic pollution.
- **DO** measures oxygen available for aquatic life. The **BIS standard for Class A waters** is **DO ≥ 6 mg/L**.
- The **DO sag curve** (Streeter-Phelps model) describes how a river's DO falls downstream of a pollution source and gradually recovers through **reaeration**. A healthy river maintains a **DO deficit of ≤ 1 mg/L**; polluted rivers may drop to **< 2 mg/L**, killing fish.

**Conclusion:** Engineers use BOD and DO as **primary design parameters** for sewage treatment plants, river restoration projects, and effluent compliance monitoring.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define the **Falkenmark Water Stress Index**. A region has an annual renewable freshwater resource of **600 × 10⁶ m³** and a population of **5 × 10⁵**. Determine whether the region is water-stressed, water-scarce, or in absolute scarcity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **major categories of water pollutants** with suitable examples. Explain the phenomenon of **eutrophication** and its environmental consequences. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Falkenmark Index Numerical (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Falkenmark Water Stress Index** is a measure of per-capita annual renewable freshwater availability, proposed by hydrologist Malin Falkenmark. It is calculated as:

$$ W_{stress} = \frac{A_{renewable}}{P} $$

where $A_{renewable}$ is the total annual renewable freshwater (m³) and $P$ is the total population.

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $A_{renewable} = 600 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 6 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3$
- $P = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ persons}$

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the Falkenmark formula.

$$ W_{stress} = \frac{6 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3}{5 \times 10^5 \text{ persons}} $$

**Step 2:** Simplify by dividing powers of 10.

$$ W_{stress} = \frac{6}{5} \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{person/year} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the result.

$$ W_{stress} = 1{,}200 \text{ m}^3/\text{person/year} $$

**Step 4:** Compare with Falkenmark thresholds (1.5 marks).

$$ 1{,}000 < 1{,}200 < 1{,}700 $$

**Step 5:** Conclude (1.5 marks).

> The region is **water-stressed** (in the range 1,000–1,700 m³/person/year). It has not yet reached scarcity, but is vulnerable to drought, climate variability, and population growth. Engineering interventions such as **rainwater harvesting, micro-irrigation, and water reuse** are recommended as a precautionary measure.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Categories of Pollutants and Eutrophication (7 Marks)

**Categories of Pollutants (4 marks):**

1. **Biological Pollutants (1 mark):** Bacteria (*E. coli, Salmonella, Vibrio cholerae*), viruses (rotavirus, hepatitis A), protozoa (*Giardia, Cryptosporidium*). Sources: sewage, animal waste. Effects: waterborne disease outbreaks.

2. **Chemical – Inorganic Pollutants (1 mark):** Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As), nitrates, fluorides, cyanides. Sources: industrial discharge, mining, agricultural runoff. Effects: cancer, kidney failure, skeletal fluorosis, blue baby syndrome.

3. **Chemical – Organic Pollutants (1 mark):** Pesticides (DDT, organochlorines, organophosphates), petroleum hydrocarbons, PCBs, dioxins. Sources: agriculture, oil spills, incineration. Effects: bioaccumulation, biomagnification, endocrine disruption, cancer.

4. **Physical Pollutants (0.5 mark):** Suspended solids, thermal discharge, plastic debris. Sources: mining, power plants, urbanization. Effects: reduced photosynthesis, habitat loss, ingestion by marine life.

5. **Emerging Pollutants (0.5 mark):** Pharmaceuticals, hormones, microplastics, PFAS, nanoparticles. Sources: domestic sewage, personal care products. Effects: antibiotic resistance, hormonal disorders, unknown long-term consequences.

**Eutrophication (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of water bodies with **nutrients (mainly nitrogen and phosphorus)**, leading to uncontrolled growth of algae and aquatic plants, depletion of dissolved oxygen, and ultimately loss of biodiversity.

**Process (1 mark):** Nutrient runoff → algal bloom → sunlight blocked → submerged plants die → bacterial decomposition → oxygen depletion → **hypoxic or anoxic zone** → fish and macroinvertebrate mortality.

**Consequences (1 mark):**
- **Ecological:** Loss of biodiversity, dominance of nuisance species, formation of **dead zones** (e.g., Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone of **> 15,000 km²**).
- **Economic:** Collapse of fisheries, reduced tourism, increased water treatment costs.
- **Health:** Cyanobacterial blooms produce **microcystins and anatoxins** that cause liver damage and are linked to neurodegenerative diseases.
- **Ethical:** Inequity — downstream and coastal communities suffer most, while upstream agricultural users benefit.

**Conclusion:** Engineers must design **constructed wetlands, riparian buffer strips, and precision agriculture systems** to reduce nutrient loads at source.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Water Scarcity & Pollution Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Omission in Falkenmark Numerical:** Forgetting to state "m³/person/year" loses **0.5–1 mark**. Always pair a number with its unit.
> 2. **Confusing BOD with COD:** BOD measures **biodegradable** organic load (5-day test), COD measures **total** oxidizable matter. Examiners penalize this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring the Ethical Dimension:** KTU 2024 Scheme is **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Answers purely technical without addressing **ethics, SDG 6, or social equity** are penalized under **CO 3 (Ethics and Sustainability)**.
> 4. **No Real-World Reference:** Generic answers without **Indian context** (e.g., Ganga, Yamuna, Kerala backwaters, Bengaluru tanker mafia) receive only partial credit. Always cite **at least one national or local case study**.
> 5. **Mislabelling Diagrams in Mermaid:** Node labels containing `|` (pipe), `*` (asterisk), or HTML tags break the Mermaid compiler. Use **plain uppercase text** only.
> 6. **Skipping Threshold Values:** Stating "water is scarce" without the **1,700 / 1,000 / 500 m³ thresholds** is treated as a vague answer. **Always cite the specific Falkenmark threshold crossed.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Water Scarcity and Pollution Issues (Module 3):**

### Water Scarcity — Key Takeaways
- **Falkenmark Thresholds:** **1,700 / 1,000 / 500 m³/person/year** — no stress / stressed / scarce / absolute.
- **India 2025:** **≈ 1,367 m³/person/year** → water-stressed.
- **Global:** **2.2 billion** people lack safely managed drinking water (UN 2024).
- **Types of Scarcity:** Physical, Economic, Temporal, Virtual.
- **Agriculture consumes ≈ 80%** of India's freshwater.
- **Climate change** worsens scarcity through glacier retreat and erratic monsoons.

### Water Pollution — Key Takeaways
- **Categories:** Biological, Chemical (Organic/Inorganic), Physical, Radiological, Emerging.
- **BIS Drinking Water Standard:** **IS 10500:2012**.
- **BOD₅ Permissible for Class A:** **≤ 3 mg/L**; **DO ≥ 6 mg/L**.
- **CPCB Industrial Discharge:** BOD ≤ **30 mg/L**, COD ≤ **250 mg/L**.
- **Eutrophication** is caused by N and P enrichment; creates hypoxic dead zones.
- **Point sources** are easy to regulate; **non-point sources** (agricultural runoff) are the toughest challenge.

### Engineering Ethics Linkages
- **SDG 6:** Clean Water and Sanitation for all by **2030**.
- **Engineer's Duty:** Truthful reporting of pollution data, design of ETPs, advocacy for low-flush toilets and rainwater harvesting.
- **Case Studies to Remember:** *Flint Water Crisis (USA, 2014–2019)* — lead poisoning from cost-cutting; *Cape Town Day Zero (2018)* — first major city to nearly run out of water; *Aral Sea disaster* — irrigation-induced desiccation; *Kerala 2018 floods* — climate-driven excess followed by water-quality collapse.

### Quick Recall Equations
- $W_{stress} = \dfrac{A_{renewable}}{P}$
- $BOD_5 = (D_1 - D_2) \times DF$
- $L = Q \times C$
- $DO_{sat} = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$

### Mnemonic for Pollutant Categories
> **"B-COPRE"** → **B**iological, **C**hemical (Organic & Inorganic), **P**hysical, **R**adiological, **E**merging.

### Last-Minute KTU Mantra
> *"Water is not an infinite resource. As engineers, we do not 'consume' it — we 'borrow' it. Our ethical duty is to return it as clean as we found it, or cleaner. That is the essence of SDG 6 and the soul of Module 3."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Water Management Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Water Management Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sustainable Water Management (SWM)** is the integrated process of planning, developing, distributing, and managing the optimum use of water resources in a manner that balances present human and ecological demands without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own water needs. It operates on the three-pillar framework of **Environmental Sustainability, Economic Viability, and Social Equity**.

The **Global Water Partnership (GWP)** formally defines the underlying framework as **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)**, which is a process that promotes the coordinated development and management of water, land, and related resources to maximise economic and social welfare equitably without compromising the sustainability of vital ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDG 6 Alignment:** Sustainable Water Management is the foundational pillar of the United Nations **Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6)** — "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all" by 2030. Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic expects explicit SDG 6 linkage.

## 1.2 Key Quantitative Metrics (Engineers Must Know)

The following indices are the **primary evaluation tools** used in the UN, World Bank, and Indian governmental reports:

| Metric | Definition | Global Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Stress Index (WSI)** | Ratio of total freshwater withdrawal to total renewable freshwater resources | < 25% Low, 25–60% Medium, > 60% High Stress |
| **Water Scarcity** | Absolute physical lack of water (< 1,700 m³/person/year) | India ≈ 1,486 m³ (Water Stressed) |
| **Water Footprint (WF)** | Total volume of freshwater used to produce goods/services | Measured in m³/capita/year |
| **Falkenmark Indicator** | Per-capita water availability threshold | < 1,000 m³ = Scarcity, < 500 m³ = Absolute Scarcity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India Status 2024:** India hosts **18% of the world's population** but possesses only **4% of global freshwater resources**, classifying it as a **"Water-Stressed"** nation under the Falkenmark indicator. This is the single most cited statistic in KTU Board Exams for this module.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Earth as a Bank Account"

Imagine the Earth's freshwater system is a **shared family bank account**:

- **Deposits** (Inflows) come from **rainfall, snowmelt, and river discharge**.
- **Withdrawals** (Outflows) are made by **agriculture (≈70%), industry (≈20%), and domestic use (≈10%)**.
- **Minimum Balance Requirement** is the **environmental flow** — water that rivers, wetlands, and aquifers *must* retain to keep the ecosystem alive.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Intuition:** A Sustainable Water Manager behaves like a careful treasurer — they never withdraw more than the natural recharge rate, they invest in "savings schemes" (recharge pits, reservoirs), they recycle "spent money" (wastewater reuse), and they ensure no one family member (sector) drains the account at the expense of others.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Sustainable Water Management (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-E Framework (Memorise This):**
> 1. **Environmental Sustainability** — Preserve hydrological cycles, maintain ecological flows, protect aquifers from over-extraction.
> 2. **Economic Viability** — Water pricing, cost recovery, efficient allocation across competing sectors (agriculture, industry, domestic).
> 3. **Social Equity (Water Justice)** — Universal access to safe drinking water, sanitation, and hygiene (**WASH**) for all, especially marginalised communities.

This 3-E framework is the **first thing** a KTU 2024 examiner expects in any 14-mark question opener.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Sustainable Water Management

Sustainable Water Management is not a single technology — it is a **decision-making system**. The following sequential logic explains how it works in practice:

### Step 1: Demand Assessment
- Quantify current withdrawals across **agricultural, industrial, and municipal (AIM)** sectors.
- Forecast future demand using population growth, GDP growth, and climate scenarios.

### Step 2: Supply Characterisation
- Map renewable freshwater resources: **surface water (rivers, lakes)** + **groundwater (aquifers)**.
- Account for the **recharge rate** versus the **extraction rate**.

### Step 3: Gap Analysis
- Calculate the **water balance deficit** (Demand − Supply).
- Identify stressed zones, especially **over-exploited blocks** (India has 1,006+ such blocks as per CGWB 2023).

### Step 4: Intervention Strategy
- **Demand-side:** Drip irrigation, water-efficient appliances, leak detection.
- **Supply-side:** Rainwater harvesting, desalination, inter-basin transfer.
- **Governance:** Participatory irrigation management, watershed committees.

### Step 5: Monitoring & Adaptive Feedback
- Use **IoT-based smart meters**, remote sensing, and **GIS-based watershed atlases** (India's Bhuvan platform is a textbook example).
- Iteratively revise strategies based on observed outcomes.

## 2.2 The Water Footprint Concept (Hoekstra's Method)

The **Water Footprint (WF)** of a product, person, or nation is the total volume of freshwater used to produce it. It is divided into three colour-coded components:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Colours of Water Footprint (Prof. Arjen Hoekstra, 2002):**
> 1. **Blue Water Footprint** — Volume of **surface and groundwater** consumed (evaporated or incorporated into a product). Example: irrigation water from a river.
> 2. **Green Water Footprint** — Volume of **rainwater** consumed. Example: soil moisture used by rain-fed crops.
> 3. **Grey Water Footprint** — Volume of freshwater required to **assimilate pollutants** to meet water quality standards. Example: fertiliser runoff from farmland.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORISE THIS TABLE** — It contains every formula that can be asked in Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part in Part B (14 marks) under the KTU 2024 Scheme.

| # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Form | Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water Stress Index | $WSI = \dfrac{W_{total}}{R_{renewable}} \times 100$ | % (dimensionless ratio) | Basin-level policy assessment |
| 2 | Falkenmark Indicator | $W_{pc} = \dfrac{R_{total}}{P_{population}}$ | m³/person/year | National scarcity classification |
| 3 | Total Water Footprint | $WF_{total} = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | m³/year or m³/capita/year | Product LCA, corporate sustainability |
| 4 | Grey Water Footprint | $WF_{grey} = \dfrac{L}{C_{max} - C_{nat}}$ | m³/unit pollutant | Industrial effluent dilution design |
| 5 | Virtual Water Content | $VW = WF_{product} \times Q_{imported}$ | m³ | International trade policy |
| 6 | Per Capita Domestic Demand | $D_{pc} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times 365}$ | Litres/person/day (LPCD) | Urban water supply design |
| 7 | Groundwater Recharge | $R_g = P \times A \times I \times F$ | mm or m³/year | Rainwater harvesting design |
| 8 | Irrigation Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{crop\ use}}{W_{applied}}$ | % | Drip vs. flood comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $W_{total}$ = total freshwater withdrawal; $R_{renewable}$ = renewable freshwater resource; $L$ = pollutant load; $C_{max}$ = max allowable concentration; $C_{nat}$ = natural background concentration; $P$ = precipitation; $A$ = area; $I$ = infiltration factor; $F$ = recharge factor.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Footprint of a Crop (Hoekstra's Method)

This is the **most asked derivation** in KTU Board Exams for this module. We will derive the water footprint of a crop in three colour components.

### Given:
- Crop yield per hectare: $Y$ (kg/ha)
- Crop water requirement: $CWR$ (mm/season)
- Effective rainfall: $P_{eff}$ (mm/season)
- Nitrogen fertiliser applied: $N$ (kg/ha)
- Natural nitrogen in runoff: $C_{nat}$ (mg/L)
- Max permissible nitrogen in water: $C_{max}$ (mg/L)

### Step 1: Blue Water Footprint (Irrigation Component)

The blue water footprint is the volume of irrigation water consumed per unit of crop produced. We first compute the **net irrigation requirement (NIR)**:

$$
NIR = CWR - P_{eff}
$$

The blue water footprint per hectare is then the NIR expressed in m³ (multiply by 10 to convert mm/ha → m³/ha):

$$
WF_{blue,ha} = NIR \times 10
$$

Finally, the **blue water footprint per kilogram of crop** is:

$$
WF_{blue} = \dfrac{WF_{blue,ha}}{Y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Logic:** $NIR$ is in mm over the field area. 1 mm over 1 hectare = 10 m³. Dividing by yield normalises to "litres of water per kg of crop".

### Step 2: Green Water Footprint (Rain-fed Component)

The green water footprint uses effective rainfall consumed by the crop:

$$
WF_{green,ha} = P_{eff} \times 10
$$

$$
WF_{green} = \dfrac{WF_{green,ha}}{Y}
$$

### Step 3: Grey Water Footprint (Pollution Assimilation)

The grey water footprint represents the volume of water needed to dilute the nitrogen fertiliser runoff to safe levels. From the formula sheet:

$$
WF_{grey} = \dfrac{L}{C_{max} - C_{nat}}
$$

Where the **pollutant load** $L$ is the nitrogen leaching fraction times the applied fertiliser:

$$
L = N \times f_{leach} \times \dfrac{10{,}000\ m^2}{1\ ha} \times \dfrac{1\ kg}{10^6\ mg}
$$

The leaching fraction $f_{leach}$ is typically assumed to be **0.10 (10%)** for nitrogen.

### Step 4: Total Water Footprint

$$
WF_{total} = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A wheat crop requires $CWR = 450$ mm over its growing season in a region receiving $P_{eff} = 200$ mm of effective rainfall. Yield $Y = 3{,}500$ kg/ha. Nitrogen fertiliser applied = 120 kg/ha. $C_{max} = 10$ mg/L, $C_{nat} = 2$ mg/L, $f_{leach} = 0.10$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step (a): Compute NIR**
$$
NIR = 450 - 200 = 250 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (b): Compute Blue Water Footprint per hectare**
$$
WF_{blue,ha} = 250 \times 10 = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{ha}
$$

**Step (c): Compute Blue Water Footprint per kg**
$$
WF_{blue} = \dfrac{2{,}500}{3{,}500} = 0.714 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \approx 714 \text{ L/kg}
$$

**Step (d): Compute Green Water Footprint per kg**
$$
WF_{green,ha} = 200 \times 10 = 2{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{ha}
$$
$$
WF_{green} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{3{,}500} = 0.571 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \approx 571 \text{ L/kg}
$$

**Step (e): Compute Grey Water Footprint**

First, compute pollutant load $L$:
$$
L = 120 \times 0.10 = 12 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

Convert to mg per hectare (1 kg = 10⁶ mg):
$$
L = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/ha} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/ha}
$$

Compute denominator:
$$
C_{max} - C_{nat} = 10 - 2 = 8 \text{ mg/L}
$$

Compute $WF_{grey}$:
$$
WF_{grey} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^7}{8} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/ha} = 1{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{ha}
$$

Per kg:
$$
WF_{grey} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{3{,}500} = 0.429 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \approx 429 \text{ L/kg}
$$

**Step (f): Total Water Footprint**
$$
WF_{total} = 714 + 571 + 429 = 1{,}714 \text{ L/kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Producing 1 kg of wheat consumes **1,714 litres of water** — a number famously used in sustainability campaigns. This single derivation appears in nearly every KTU Board Exam paper.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Groundwater Recharge Estimate (Empirical Formula)

For a rainfall event over a watershed, the empirical groundwater recharge formula is:

$$
R_g = P \times A \times I \times F
$$

Where:
- $R_g$ = groundwater recharge (m³)
- $P$ = precipitation depth (m)
- $A$ = watershed area (m²)
- $I$ = infiltration factor (dimensionless, 0.2–0.4 for most soils)
- $F$ = recharge factor (0.05–0.15 for alluvial aquifers)

### Analytical Derivation:

Starting from the water balance for an unconfined aquifer:

$$
R_g = P - R_{surface} - ET - \Delta S_{soil}
$$

Where $R_{surface}$ is surface runoff, $ET$ is evapotranspiration, and $\Delta S_{soil}$ is change in soil moisture. Empirically:

$$
R_{surface} = P \times (1 - I)
$$
$$
ET + \Delta S_{soil} = P \times (1 - F)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
R_g = P - P(1 - I) - P(1 - F) = P \times I \times F
$$

Multiplying by area $A$ (in m²) and precipitation depth $P$ (in m) yields the volumetric recharge:

$$
R_g = P \times A \times I \times F
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A rooftop of $150$ m² receives $P = 1.2$ m of annual rainfall. Given $I = 0.30$ and $F = 0.10$, compute the annual groundwater recharge.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
R_g = 1.2 \times 150 \times 0.30 \times 0.10
$$
$$
R_g = 5.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Significance:** This 5.4 m³/year is the design basis for rooftop rainwater harvesting recharge pits. A typical recharge pit of 1.5 m depth × 1.0 m diameter has a volume of ≈ 1.77 m³, and replenishes completely in ≈ 4 months.

## 4.1 The IWRM Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)** framework, which is the operational backbone of all sustainable water management systems globally.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INPUT LAYER Rainfall Surface Inflow Groundwater Recharge] --> B[IWRM CORE SYSTEM]
    C[Human Demand Agricultural Industrial Municipal] --> B
    D[Environmental Flow Minimum Ecological Requirement] --> B

    B --> E{DEMAND SUPPLY BALANCE CHECK}
    E -->|Deficit Detected| F[INTERVENTION ENGINE]
    E -->|Surplus Available| G[STORAGE AND DISTRIBUTION]
    E -->|Balanced| H[STATUS QUO MONITORING]

    F --> F1[Demand Side Drip Irrigation Water Efficient Appliances Leak Detection]
    F --> F2[Supply Side Rainwater Harvesting Desalination Inter Basin Transfer]
    F --> F3[Governance Pricing Reforms Participatory Committees WASH Access]

    G --> I[OUTPUT LAYER]
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I

    I --> J[Equitable Water Distribution]
    I --> K[Ecological Sustainability]
    I --> L[Economic Productivity]

    I --> M[FEEDBACK LOOP IoT Sensors Satellite Data GIS Atlas CGWB Reports]
    M --> E

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A68,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#E27D4A,stroke:#8C3B1A,color:#FFFFFF

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows how IWRM operates as a closed-loop control system. Rainfall and human demand enter the core system, which checks the balance. If a deficit exists, an intervention engine activates three parallel solutions (demand-side, supply-side, governance). The output is monitored via IoT sensors and satellite data, feeding back into the system — this is the **adaptive management** principle of IWRM.

## 4.2 The Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rainfall on Rooftop] --> R2[Gutter and Downpipe]

    style R1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A68,color:#FFFFFF

    style R9 fill:#417505,stroke:#1F3A02,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Components Explained:**
> - **First Flush Diverter:** Diverts the first 10–20 minutes of rainfall, which carries rooftop dust, bird droppings, and pollutants.
> - **Filter Unit:** Multi-layer bed of sand, gravel, and activated charcoal removes suspended solids and improves water quality.
> - **Recharge Pit:** A pit filled with boulders and sand that allows filtered water to percolate into the aquifer, raising the local water table.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Nexus Insight:** Water, Energy, and Food are the three pillars of human survival, and they are **inseparable**. For example, growing food requires water (irrigation) and energy (pumping, fertilisers); producing energy requires water (cooling); and securing water often requires energy (pumping, desalination). Sustainable water management **cannot be planned in isolation** — it must consider these feedback loops.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions test direct recall and brief application. Each answer should be **structured in 3 paragraphs**: definition, key features, and a one-line example.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]
**"Define Sustainable Water Management and list its three pillars."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Sustainable Water Management (SWM) refers to the integrated planning, development, and use of water resources in a way that meets present human and ecological demands without compromising the ability of future generations to satisfy their own needs. It is operationalised through the **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)** framework.

**Three Pillars:** The 3-E framework of SWM consists of:
1. **Environmental Sustainability** — protecting aquifers, rivers, and ecosystems from over-extraction and pollution.
2. **Economic Viability** — efficient pricing, cost recovery, and optimal allocation across agricultural, industrial, and domestic sectors.
3. **Social Equity** — universal access to safe drinking water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH), particularly for vulnerable communities.

**Example:** India's **Jal Jeevan Mission (2019)** aims to provide 55 litres per capita per day (LPCD) of potable water to every rural household through tap connections — exemplifying the equity pillar.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**"Differentiate between Water Footprint and Virtual Water."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Water Footprint | Virtual Water |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Total freshwater consumed (direct + indirect) to produce a good/service | The water "embedded" in a product, traded implicitly when the product is imported/exported |
| **Scope** | Measured at producer level | Measured at trade level |
| **Unit** | m³/capita/year or m³/tonne | m³/tonne of traded commodity |
| **Focus** | Consumption accounting | Trade policy and global water savings |

**Example:** When India imports 1 tonne of wheat from Australia (≈ 1,714 m³ water footprint per tonne), it "imports" the **virtual water** of 1,714 m³, saving its own domestic water resources. Virtual water trade is therefore a **geopolitical tool** for water-scarce nations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** between two sub-options. Each sub-question is typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Explain the concept of Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) in detail. Discuss its key principles, the role of stakeholder participation, and its implementation challenges in the Indian context."**

**Mark Distribution:**
- Part (a): **7 marks** — Concept + Principles
- Part (b): **7 marks** — Stakeholder participation + Indian challenges

#### Part (a) — Concept and Principles of IWRM (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Definition and Origin (2 marks)**
IWRM is defined by the **Global Water Partnership (GWP, 2000)** as "a process which promotes the coordinated development and management of water, land, and related resources in order to maximise the resultant economic and social welfare in an equitable manner without compromising the sustainability of vital ecosystems." It emerged from the **Dublin Principles (1992)** and **Agenda 21 (Rio Earth Summit, 1992)**.

**Step 2: The Four Dublin Principles (3 marks)**
1. **Freshwater is a finite and vulnerable resource, essential to sustain life, development, and the environment.**
2. **Water development and management should be based on a participatory approach, involving users, planners, and policymakers at all levels.**
3. **Women play a central part in the provision, management, and safeguarding of water.**
4. **Water has an economic value in all its competing uses and should be recognised as an economic good.**

**Step 3: Pillars of IWRM (2 marks)**
The operational pillars are:
- **Water resource management** (technical and hydrological)
- **Water service delivery** (infrastructure and utilities)
- **Water governance and institutions** (policy, law, finance)

#### Part (b) — Stakeholder Participation and Indian Implementation Challenges (7 Marks)

**Step 4: Stakeholder Participation (3 marks)**
Stakeholder participation involves **water users associations (WUAs), panchayati raj institutions (PRIs), NGOs, the private sector, and women self-help groups (SHGs)**. The **Pani Panchayat** model in Odisha and **Jal Swaraj** model in Maharashtra are textbook examples of participatory watershed management. Stakeholder participation ensures equity, ownership, and sustainability of water projects.

**Step 5: Indian Implementation Challenges (4 marks)**
1. **Fragmented Governance:** Water is a **State Subject (Entry 17, List II)** under the Indian Constitution, leading to inter-state river disputes (e.g., Krishna, Kaveri, Ravi waters).
2. **Groundwater Over-extraction:** India is the **largest groundwater user globally** (~230 billion m³/year), with over 1,000 blocks classified as "over-exploited" by the Central Ground Water Board (CGWB 2023).
3. **Inefficient Agriculture:** Flood irrigation consumes ~80% of freshwater but contributes only ~15% of agricultural GDP, demanding urgent shift to micro-irrigation.
4. **Climate Change Impact:** Erratic monsoons, glacier retreat in the Himalayas, and rising sea levels threaten both surface and groundwater quality.
5. **Pricing and Subsidies:** Highly subsidised electricity for pumps leads to wasteful extraction, distorting the economic pillar of IWRM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students write about IWRM **without naming the Dublin Principles** (1992). This alone costs **2 marks** in Part (a). Memorise all four principles verbatim.

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Define Water Footprint. With a suitable numerical example, explain the three components (blue, green, grey) of the water footprint of an agricultural crop. Discuss the engineering strategies for reducing water footprint in urban areas."**

**Mark Distribution:**
- Part (a): **7 marks** — Definition + Numerical example
- Part (b): **7 marks** — Urban water-footprint reduction strategies

#### Part (a) — Water Footprint Definition and Numerical Example (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
The **Water Footprint (WF)**, introduced by Prof. **Arjen Hoekstra (2002)** at the University of Twente, Netherlands, is the total volume of freshwater used directly or indirectly by a person, community, business, or nation. It is measured in m³/year (per capita) or m³/tonne (per product).

**Step 2: Three Components (3 marks)**
- **Blue WF:** Consumption of surface and groundwater resources.
- **Green WF:** Consumption of rainwater stored in soil.
- **Grey WF:** Volume required to dilute pollutants to safe standards.

**Step 3: Numerical Example (3 marks)**
*Recalling the wheat example from Section 3.1:*
For a wheat crop with $CWR = 450$ mm, $P_{eff} = 200$ mm, $Y = 3{,}500$ kg/ha, and $N = 120$ kg/ha:
$$
WF_{blue} = 714 \text{ L/kg},\ \ WF_{green} = 571 \text{ L/kg},\ \ WF_{grey} = 429 \text{ L/kg}
$$
$$
WF_{total} = 1{,}714 \text{ L/kg}
$$
This is the **1,714 litres of water per kg of wheat** figure frequently cited in sustainability reports.

#### Part (b) — Urban Water Footprint Reduction Strategies (7 Marks)

**Step 4: Demand-Side Strategies (3 marks)**
1. **Low-flow Fixtures:** Aerator taps, dual-flush toilets, BEE 5-star rated washing machines and dishwashers can reduce household water use by **30–40%**.
2. **Leak Detection:** Smart water meters (e.g., Itron, Kamstrup) and acoustic sensors detect pipeline leaks, reducing non-revenue water (NRW) which averages **40%** in Indian urban utilities.
3. **Rainwater Harvesting:** Mandatory in many Indian states (Tamil Nadu was the first in 2003); rooftop RWH can meet **20–50%** of domestic non-potable demand.

**Step 5: Supply-Side and Reuse Strategies (3 marks)**
1. **Greywater Recycling:** Treating wastewater from showers and washing machines for toilet flushing and gardening can reduce freshwater demand by **30–40%**.
2. **Decentralised STPs:** Small-scale sewage treatment plants in apartment complexes recycle water for landscaping and car washing.
3. **Smart Irrigation:** Soil-moisture-sensor-based irrigation in urban parks prevents over-watering.

**Step 6: Policy Strategies (1 mark)**
- **Water Pricing:** Tiered pricing penalises wasteful use.
- **Building Codes:** National Building Code (NBC 2016) mandates RWH for all new buildings > 300 m².
- **Public Awareness:** Campaigns like **Jal Shakti Abhiyan** (2019) and **Catch the Rain** (2021).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to **only mention rainwater harvesting** for urban water reduction. To score full marks, you must include **greywater recycling, smart metering, leak reduction, and policy instruments** — all four categories. Mentioning just one or two limits the answer to 3–4 marks.

---

- [ ] **SDG 6.6:** Protect water-related ecosystems (mountains, forests, wetlands, rivers)

### Engineering Strategies to Remember
- [ ] **Drip irrigation** → 30–50% water savings
- [ ] **Rooftop RWH** → 20–50% of non-potable demand
- [ ] **Greywater recycling** → 30–40% household water reuse
- [ ] **Desalination** → Coastal cities only (high energy cost)
- [ ] **Watershed management** → Catchment-level runoff capture

### Diagram Recall Test
- [ ] Sketch the IWRM block diagram (Input → Core → Output → Feedback)
- [ ] Sketch the Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting flow (Catchment → Gutter → Filter → Storage/Recharge)
- [ ] Sketch the Water-Energy-Food Nexus triangle

### Common 14-Mark Question Patterns
1. "Explain IWRM with Dublin Principles" — Use Question A structure
2. "Water Footprint with numerical example" — Use Question B structure
3. "Rainwater harvesting design and benefits" — Use Section 4.2
4. "Water-Energy-Food Nexus and its policy implications"
5. "Case study of Jal Jeevan Mission / Atal Mission for Rejuvenation (AMRUT)"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start every 14-mark answer with a **2-line definition + 3 key features**, then proceed to the sub-parts. End with a **concluding paragraph** linking to SDG 6 or India's policy framework. This structure is the KTU 2024 Board Examiner's gold standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Environmental Flow (E-Flow) Definition**
> **Environmental Flow** is the quantity, timing, frequency, duration, and quality of water flows required to sustain freshwater and estuarine ecosystems, the human livelihoods, and the well-being of communities that depend on these aquatic ecosystems. It is also referred to as **Environmental Water Allocation (EWA)** or **Environmental Water Requirement (EWR)** in contemporary water-resources literature.

The **Brisbane Declaration (2007)** formally defines Environmental Flows as:
> *"The quantity, timing, frequency, duration and rate of change of water flows required to maintain the components, functions, services and uses of aquatic ecosystems."*

In the context of the **National Water Policy (2012)** of India, environmental flows are recognized as a non-negotiable allocation prior to any consumptive or allocative use, ensuring that **minimum ecological flows** are preserved even in regulated river systems.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE] **The "Lungs of a River" Analogy**
> Imagine a river is like the **lungs of the planet** — just as humans need a minimum flow of oxygen to survive, a river needs a minimum flow of water to sustain its biodiversity (fish, aquatic plants, insects, microbes, birds, and riparian forests).

> Think of a river as a **bank account**:
> - **Deposits** = rainfall, snowmelt, groundwater inflow
> - **Withdrawals** = drinking water, irrigation, industries, hydropower
> - **Minimum Balance** = Environmental Flow
>
> Just as a bank won't allow you to withdraw your entire savings (or you incur penalties), a river system cannot have **100% of its water diverted** for human use — it must retain a *minimum ecological reserve* so that the river continues to "breathe", migrate fish, transport sediments, and recharge groundwater.

> If a river is drained below its **ecological threshold**, it becomes a **drainage ditch** — losing its capacity to support life, regulate climate, sustain fisheries, and recharge aquifers.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology — Syllabus Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **E-Flow** | Environmental Flow — water reserved for ecosystem health |
| **MAF** | Mean Annual Flow of the river basin |
| **EWA** | Environmental Water Allocation |
| **EWR** | Environmental Water Requirement |
| **IFIM** | Instream Flow Incremental Methodology |
| **PHABSIM** | Physical Habitat Simulation System |
| **MFD** | Modified Flush Flow Discharge |
| **FDC** | Flow Duration Curve |
| **HP / MW** | Hydropower (engineering context) |
| **RoR** | Run-of-River (hydropower projects) |

---

### 1.4 Why Environmental Flow? — The Ethical Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Ethics Connection (UCHUT347 Core)**
> Environmental Flow is a textbook case of **intergenerational equity**, **environmental justice**, and **sustainable engineering**. It forces engineers and policymakers to answer:
> *Is it ethically defensible to divert 100% of a river for industrial/agricultural use if it destroys the ecosystem that sustains millions of downstream dependents, including future generations?*

The **three pillars of sustainable development** intersect here:
1. **Economic** — irrigation, hydropower, industry
2. **Social** — drinking water, fisheries, livelihood
3. **Environmental** — biodiversity, flood control, groundwater recharge, climate regulation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Environmental Flow

A healthy river needs not just *one* flow, but a **regime of flows** throughout the year:

| Component | Function | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low Flows (Base Flows)** | Sustain aquatic life during dry seasons | 10% to 30% of MAF |
| **High Flows (Freshes)** | Trigger fish spawning, wetland connectivity | 30% to 60% of MAF |
| **Bankfull Flows** | Maintain channel shape, sediment transport | 60% to 100% of MAF |
| **Flushing / Overbank Flows** | Rejuvenate floodplains, flush pollutants, nutrients | > 100% of MAF (floods) |

---

### 2.2 Methods to Determine Environmental Flow

#### A. **Hydrological Methods (Desktop / Index Methods)**

These use historical flow data without detailed biological fieldwork. Fastest and most commonly used in KTU/engineering assessments.

##### (i) **Tennant Method (Montana Method, 1976)**
Donald Tennant of the US Fish & Wildlife Service proposed a percentage-based classification of Mean Annual Flow (MAF).

| Flow Class (\% of MAF) | Habitat Quality |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 10% | Severe degradation — only opportunistic species survive |
| 10 – 30% | Maintaining — suitable for short-term survival |
| 30 – 60% | Good / Optimum — provides excellent habitat for most aquatic life |
| 60 – 100% | Outstanding / Bankfull — flushing flows, floodplain connectivity |
| > 100% | Flushing / Flood flows — floodplain rejuvenation |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Rule of Thumb**
> For most KTU exam problems: **E-Flow = 30% of MAF for optimum habitat**, **10% of MAF as absolute minimum**.

##### (ii) **Flow Duration Curve (FDC) Method**
A graphical technique where **Q (discharge)** is plotted against **% of time it is equalled or exceeded**. The Q90 (flow exceeded 90% of the time) is taken as a candidate environmental flow.

##### (iii) **Q50 / Q75 / Q90 Index Methods**
- **Q50** = flow exceeded 50% of the time (median flow)
- **Q75** = flow exceeded 75% of the time
- **Q90** = flow exceeded 90% of the time — typically the recommended minimum E-Flow

#### B. **Hydraulic Rating Methods**
These use river geometry rather than biology directly.

##### (i) **Wetted Perimeter Method (Montana / Annear Method)**
Plots wetted perimeter (the length of riverbed submerged) versus discharge. The **inflection point** of the curve — where wetted perimeter stops increasing rapidly — is identified as the minimum E-Flow. Beyond this point, increasing discharge yields little habitat benefit.

#### C. **Habitat Simulation Methods**

##### (i) **PHABSIM (Physical Habitat Simulation)**
Models the relationship between flow and **Weighted Usable Area (WUA)** for target species (e.g., Mahseer in Indian rivers).

#### D. **Holistic / Ecosystem Methods**
- **Brisbane Declaration framework**
- **DRIFT (Downstream Response to Imposed Flow Transformations)** — used in South Africa
- **Building Block Flow Methodology (BBFM)** — Australia

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and based on standard hydrology texts (Poff et al., 1997; Tennant, 1976; Annear et al., 2002).

| Method | Formula | Where It Is Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tennant (Montana)** | $Q_{e} = \dfrac{p}{100} \times Q_{MAF}$ | Quick environmental allocation |
| **FDC-based** | $Q_{e} = Q_{90}$ (or $Q_{75}$) | Long-term flow records available |
| **Wet Season E-Flow** | $Q_{e,\text{wet}} = 0.30 \cdot Q_{MAF}$ | Monsoon months (June–Oct) |
| **Dry Season E-Flow** | $Q_{e,\text{dry}} = 0.10 \cdot Q_{MAF}$ | Non-monsoon months (Nov–May) |
| **Power Loss (Hydro)** | $\Delta P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \Delta Q \cdot H$ | Trade-off analysis with hydropower |
| **Weighted Usable Area** | $WUA = \sum_{i} A_i \cdot SI_i$ | Habitat suitability modelling |
| **Run-of-River Design** | $Q_{\text{design}} = Q_{MAF} - Q_{e}$ | Small hydropower projects |
| **Ecological Reserve** | $R_{eco} = 0.30 \cdot Q_{MAF}$ | Default policy floor in India |

Where:
- $Q_{e}$ = environmental flow discharge
- $Q_{MAF}$ = mean annual flow
- $p$ = percentage as per Tennant table
- $Q_{90}$ = flow exceeded 90% of time on FDC
- $\eta$ = turbine efficiency
- $\rho$ = density of water (1000 $\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $g$ = 9.81 $\text{m/s}^2$
- $H$ = head in metres
- $SI_i$ = suitability index for species $i$
- $A_i$ = area of micro-habitat unit $i$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Environmental flow assessment is **mandatory** in:
- **EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment)** studies under the EIA Notification, 2006 (amended 2020).
- **Hydropower projects** — particularly Run-of-River (RoR) schemes on Himalayan rivers.
- **Inter-State Water Disputes** — Krishna, Kaveri, Narmada Tribunal awards.
- **World Bank / ADB funded** dam and irrigation projects.
- **Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS)** in urban planning.

In India, the **Central Water Commission (CWC)** recommends that 15% to 20% of the mean lean-season flow must be maintained as environmental flow for inter-state rivers, while the **MoEFCC** guidelines recommend 30% of MAF as a holistic target.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tennant Method for Environmental Flow

**Problem Setup (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A river in Kerala has a Mean Annual Flow (MAF) of 1200 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the Environmental Flow required for:
> (a) Optimum habitat during the wet season
> (b) Minimum survival flow during the dry season

---

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Tennant percentage from the standard table.**

From the Tennant classification:
- Optimum habitat = **30%** of MAF
- Minimum (survival) = **10%** of MAF

**Step 2 — Apply the Tennant formula.**

$$Q_{e} = \frac{p}{100} \times Q_{MAF}$$

Substituting:

$$Q_{e,\text{opt}} = \frac{30}{100} \times 1200$$

$$Q_{e,\text{opt}} = 0.30 \times 1200$$

$$Q_{e,\text{opt}} = 360 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum flow.**

$$Q_{e,\text{min}} = \frac{10}{100} \times 1200$$

$$Q_{e,\text{min}} = 0.10 \times 1200$$

$$Q_{e,\text{min}} = 120 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Optimum habitat E-Flow = **360 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$**
- Minimum survival E-Flow = **120 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$**

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** If a proposed dam on this river diverts >840 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (i.e., leaves <360 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$), it is in **violation of sustainable engineering ethics** and MoEFCC norms.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydropower Trade-Off When E-Flow Is Mandated

**Problem Setup:**
> A Run-of-River hydropower project on a Kerala river has:
> - Mean Annual Flow $Q_{MAF} = 800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
> - Net Head $H = 25 \; \text{m}$
> - Turbine efficiency $\eta = 0.85$
> - Mandated environmental flow = 30% of MAF
>
> Calculate: (a) Power loss in kW due to E-Flow release (b) % reduction in installed capacity.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the environmental flow release.**

$$Q_{e} = 0.30 \times 800$$

$$Q_{e} = 240 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the usable flow for power.**

$$Q_{\text{usable}} = Q_{MAF} - Q_{e}$$

$$Q_{\text{usable}} = 800 - 240$$

$$Q_{\text{usable}} = 560 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Installed Capacity (Full Flow).**

Using the standard hydropower equation:

$$P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

Where:
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

$$P_{\text{full}} = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 800 \times 25$$

$$P_{\text{full}} = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 20{,}000$$

$$P_{\text{full}} = 166{,}770{,}000 \; \text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{full}} = 166{,}770 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Power After E-Flow Release.**

$$P_{\text{usable}} = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 560 \times 25$$

$$P_{\text{usable}} = 116{,}739{,}000 \; \text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{usable}} = 116{,}739 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Power Lost (the "ecological cost").**

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{full}} - P_{\text{usable}}$$

$$\Delta P = 166{,}770 - 116{,}739$$

$$\Delta P = 50{,}031 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Percentage Reduction.**

$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{\text{full}}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{50{,}031}{166{,}770} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Reduction} \approx 30\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Conclusion:** Releasing 30% of MAF as environmental flow leads to a **30% reduction in installed capacity** for RoR projects — this is the **ethical trade-off** engineers must justify to stakeholders. Hydropower companies often lobby for lower E-Flows (10%–15%) citing economic losses, while ecologists demand higher (40%–60%) for fish migration.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Methods

| Criterion | Tennant | FDC (Q90) | Wetted Perimeter | PHABSIM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Needed** | MAF only | Daily flow record | Cross-section surveys | Flow + bathymetry + biology |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Time** | Hours | Days | Weeks | Months |
| **Biologically robust?** | No | No | Partially | Yes |
| **Used in Indian EIA?** | Yes (initial) | Yes (CWC) | Yes (specific sites) | Rare (only large dams) |
| **KTU Exam Relevance** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Weighted Usable Area (WUA) Concept

The **WUA** quantifies the *effective* river area available for a target species.

**Formula:**

$$WUA = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \times SI_i$$

Where:
- $A_i$ = area of micro-habitat cell $i$ (in $\text{m}^2$)
- $SI_i$ = Suitability Index for cell $i$, ranging from 0 (unsuitable) to 1 (optimal)
- $n$ = total number of cells

**Example (KTU-style):**
> A 1 km river stretch is divided into 10 cells. The discharge is varied to identify the optimum flow. At $Q = 50 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, the suitability indices for Mahseer (Tor putitora) are: 0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0, 0.9, 0.6, 0.2. Each cell has area $100 \; \text{m}^2$. Calculate the WUA.

**Solution:**

$$WUA = 100 \times (0.0 + 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.9 + 1.0 + 0.9 + 0.6 + 0.2)$$

$$WUA = 100 \times 5.2$$

$$WUA = 520 \; \text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The flow of 50 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ provides 520 $\text{m}^2$ of usable Mahseer habitat in this stretch. Engineers then vary $Q$ (e.g., 30, 50, 70, 100 $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) and plot WUA vs Q — the peak of this curve indicates the **biologically optimum environmental flow**.

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive E-Flow Decision Matrix

| Step | Question | If YES | If NO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Is reliable daily flow data available for ≥20 years? | Use FDC (Q90 / Q75) method | Use Tennant as proxy |
| 2 | Is the river a major inter-state / ecological hotspot? | Use PHABSIM / Holistic methods | Hydrological method acceptable |
| 3 | Is a major dam or RoR project proposed? | Mandate 30% of MAF (MoEFCC) | At least 10% survival flow |
| 4 | Are endemic / migratory species present (e.g., Hilsa, Mahseer)? | Use species-specific WUA | Generic methods suffice |
| 5 | Will the project reduce >50% of lean-season flow? | Mandatory downstream impact study | Mitigation plan adequate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
