# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Assessment pattern reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE, 100 marks full-syllabus, 3-hr):**
> - Part A: 2-mark short questions (×10).
> - Part B: 14-mark long-answer questions with internal choice (5 modules × 1 = 5 questions, attempt 5).
> - Per-module focus: one full 14-mark question × 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each.
> - Cognitive levels tested: L1 Remember, L2 Understand, L3 Apply, L4 Analyse (Bloom's Revised, Anderson & Krathwohl 2001 — used by KTU).

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define Sustainable Urban Mobility. State any three characteristics.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Sustainable Urban Mobility refers to the provision of transport systems that meet the current accessibility and mobility needs of urban residents *without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own*. It is operationalised through the **Sustainable Urban Mobility Plan (SUMP)**.

**Three characteristics (3 × 1 mark each, any 3):**
1. **Environmental** — minimises GHG emissions, air and noise pollution.
2. **Social / Equitable** — inclusive access for all income groups, ages, genders, and abilities.
3. **Economic** — efficient use of public funds, low externalities.
4. **Integrated** — combines land use and transport planning (TOD).
5. **Multi-modal** — gives priority to walking, cycling, and public transport.

### Q2. What is the "Avoid–Shift–Improve" hierarchy? Why is "Avoid" prioritised over "Improve"?  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ASI (1 mark):** A three-tier policy hierarchy for sustainable urban mobility. *Avoid* reduces the need to travel; *Shift* moves trips to sustainable modes; *Improve* makes remaining motorised trips cleaner.
- **Prioritisation reason (2 marks):** Avoid (compact city, tele-work) is the most cost-effective intervention per tonne of CO₂ avoided; Improve (e.g., retrofitting to EVs) is the *last* resort because it is the most capital-intensive and only mitigates — it does not eliminate — the underlying motorised trip. Hence Avoid > Shift > Improve in policy sequencing.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer / ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Per KTU ESE pattern:** each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B for your chosen module.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Sustainable Urban Mobility Plan – Components and Application

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 variant]`

#### (a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-phase life-cycle of a Sustainable Urban Mobility Plan (SUMP)** as defined by the European Commission / *Eltis*. List the key stakeholders in each phase.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A SUMP is a long-term, integrated transport plan for a city that addresses all modes and all transport objectives. The EC/*Eltis* methodology defines a four-phase life cycle.

**The four phases (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Phase 1 — Set-up & Analysis of the current mobility situation.**
   - Stakeholders: Municipal corporation, urban-planning department, MoHUA, IIT/NIT academic partners, civil-society NGOs.
   - Deliverables: Existing modal-share survey, accident-data analysis, air-quality baseline.

2. **Phase 2 — Strategy Development.**
   - Stakeholders: City mayor, elected representatives, public transport operators, employer associations, citizen panels.
   - Deliverables: Vision, measurable objectives (e.g., +20% PT share, −30% PM2.5), ASI policy bundles.

3. **Phase 3 — Measure & Measure Package Planning.**
   - Stakeholders: Engineers (civil, electrical, transport), contractors, traffic police, accessibility-advocacy groups.
   - Deliverables: Detailed design of BRT corridors, cycle tracks, pedestrianisation, ITS.

4. **Phase 4 — Implementation, Monitoring & Review.**
   - Stakeholders: Smart-City SPV, regulatory bodies (NHAI, NGT), citizen-feedback portals.
   - Deliverables: KPIs (ridership, air quality, fatalities), annual review, mid-cycle correction.

**Block diagram reference (2 marks):** Sketch a four-block vertical flow: `Phase 1 → Phase 2 → Phase 3 → Phase 4`, with a **feedback loop** from Phase 4 back to Phase 1, indicating the *iterative* nature of the plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's insight:** Most students draw only the forward arrows and **miss the feedback loop**, losing 1 mark. Always show the cyclic nature.

#### (b) [7 marks] Apply the **Avoid–Shift–Improve** framework to a real Indian city of your choice (e.g., Kochi, Bengaluru, Pune, Chennai) and propose **two specific engineering interventions per tier**, justifying them with at least one quantitative metric each.

**Model Answer (7 marks — illustrative for Bengaluru, adapt for any city):**

**City context (1 mark):** Bengaluru has a private-car-and-two-wheeler modal share > 70%, average commute of 12 km, PM2.5 ≈ 50 µg/m³, severe congestion on ORR.

**AVOID tier (2 marks):**
- *Intervention 1:* **Decentralised office hubs in ORR-adjacent townships** to cut commute distance. Justification: a 30% reduction in average commute distance cuts vehicle-km travelled by 30% → ≈ 4.5 million veh-km/day avoided.
- *Intervention 2:* **Mandatory work-from-home 2 days/week for IT-BT companies**, codified by the Karnataka State IT policy. Justification: ~10% of 1.4 million IT employees × 30 daily veh-km = 4.2 million veh-km/day avoided.

**SHIFT tier (2 marks):**
- *Intervention 3:* **Operationalise the 300-km Bengaluru Suburban Rail (K-RIDE)** for shift from car/2W to rail. Justification: 1.5 lakh car commuters × 24 km × 150 gCO₂/pkm = **540 t CO₂/day avoided** (refer to Section 3.2 derivation logic).
- *Intervention 4:* **Concessionary parking pricing** — ₹ 50/hr in core areas to discourage long-duration car parking. Justification: empirically reduces car trips by 10–15% (London CC zone study, 2003).

**IMPROVE tier (2 marks):**
- *Intervention 5:* **100% electric BMTC bus fleet by 2030** (per Bangalore Electric Vehicle and Energy Storage Policy 2017). Justification: cuts gCO₂/pkm of bus from 70 to 25 g → ~1.3 lakh t CO₂/yr avoided (with 6500 buses × 200 km/day × 300 days × 45 g/pkm × 100 passengers).
- *Intervention 6:* **AI-based adaptive traffic signals on 100 junctions** (B-Trac, 2023). Justification: reduces average wait time by 25%, stops by 18% (Bengaluru Smart City pilot data), with downstream emission benefit of ~5–8% on optimised corridors.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A balanced ASI portfolio — not only EV adoption — is the **only** sustainable path for a high-growth Indian megacity.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Mass Rapid Transit and the 15-Minute City — A Sustainability Analysis

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 variant — INTERNAL CHOICE]`

#### (a) [7 marks] Compare **Metro Rail, BRT, and Monorail** as sustainable urban-mobility solutions under the **five-criterion analytical matrix** (Accessibility, Environmental Load, Economic Efficiency, Safety, Resilience). Recommend the most suitable mode for a **medium-density Tier-2 Indian city** (population ≈ 1.5–3 million).

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Metro Rail | BRT | Monorail | Best |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accessibility** (catchment) | 500 m walkshed; 1 line covers ~25 km | 500 m walkshed; 1 corridor covers ~20 km; flexible extensions | 500 m walkshed; structural (elevated) | Metro ≈ BRT > Monorail |
| **Environmental Load (gCO₂/pkm)** | 25–40 | 50–70 | 40–60 | **Metro best** |
| **Economic Efficiency (capex ₹/km)** | 200–400 Cr | 30–60 Cr | 100–150 Cr | **BRT best** |
| **Safety** | Very high (grade-separated) | Moderate (at-grade; needs segregated lane) | High (elevated, no crossings) | Metro ≈ Monorail |
| **Resilience / Scalability** | Limited extensions; rigid | High — routes, headway, vehicle type all flexible | Moderate | **BRT best** |

**Recommendation (2 marks):** A medium-density Tier-2 city (e.g., **Kochi, Coimbatore, Indore, Bhopal**) should start with a **BRT spine (or electric-bus trunk + metro feeder)**, complemented by **dedicated cycle tracks and pedestrianisation**. Metro may be added later when the BRT spine exceeds 8 000 pphpd. Monorail is **not recommended** for Tier-2 cities due to its limited capacity and high maintenance complexity (e.g., the Mumbai Monorail's under-performance is a frequently cited case study).

#### (b) [7 marks] Explain the **15-Minute City concept** in detail. Show, with a calculation, that the **cycling catchment** of a 15-minute journey is roughly **3.75 km**, while the **walking catchment** is **1.25 km**. Discuss how this concept complements Transit-Oriented Development.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept definition (2 marks):** Proposed by Carlos Moreno (2016) and adopted by Paris (Mayor Hidalgo, 2020), the 15-minute city aims to enable every resident to reach six essential urban functions — *work, education, healthcare, commerce, recreation, and basic services* — within a 15-minute walk or cycle from their home. It is a *chrono-urbanist* model where **time**, not distance, is the equity metric.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Walking speed** $v_w$ = **5 km/h** (typical adult pedestrian, TRB Highway Capacity Manual).
**Cycling speed** $v_c$ = **15 km/h** (typical urban commuter, ITDP data).

For 15 min = **0.25 h**:

$$
r_{walk} \;=\; v_w \cdot 0.25 \;=\; 5 \times 0.25 \;=\; 1.25 \text{ km}
$$

$$
r_{cycle} \;=\; v_c \cdot 0.25 \;=\; 15 \times 0.25 \;=\; 3.75 \text{ km}
$$

**Result:** Walking catchment ≈ 1.25 km, Cycling catchment ≈ 3.75 km — both consistent with the Reference Sheet Equation 10.

**Complementarity with TOD (2 marks):**
- The 15-minute city is a **micro-scale** concept (neighbourhood level), while TOD is a **macro-scale** concept (city level). They are **complementary, not competing**.
- A complete sustainable city design **layers** the two: at the *neighbourhood* level, the 15-minute city ensures daily essentials are walkable; at the *city* level, TOD ensures a metro/BRT station is accessible (typically by cycle → 3.75 km = matches a typical 4-km TOD edge).
- Together, they deliver the **Avoid** tier of the ASI hierarchy: short, non-motorised trips replace motorised ones.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Module 3 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SUMP with SUTP (Sustainable Urban Transport Plan)** — they are interchangeable, but if the examiner specifically asks for SUMP, the 4-phase EC/Eltis structure is expected.
> 2. **Quoting modal-share values without units (%)** — always include the percent sign; examiners often deduct a half-mark for this.
> 3. **Citing pphpd as just "passengers per hour"** — the *direction* (per direction) is **mandatory** in transit design.
> 4. **Treating EVs as a complete sustainability solution** — examiners are explicitly trained (per NEP 2020) to award marks for *system-level thinking* (EV + grid + charging + equity). A one-line "EV = green" answer will fetch at most 2/14.
> 5. **Numerical errors in BCR calculations** — confusing *annuity factor* $(1 - (1+r)^{-T})/r$ with discount-only factor $(1+r)^{-T}$ is the most common algebraic error.
> 6. **Missing the equity/ethics angle** — every Module 3 question in the KTU 2024 scheme has a *hidden* ethics sub-aspect (gender, disability, low-income). Not addressing it can cost ~2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density-packed final revision — memorise in this exact sequence.**

- **SUMP** = Strategic plan, 4 phases (Set-up → Strategy → Measures → Implement/Review), iterative, *Eltis* methodology.
- **ASI hierarchy** = **Avoid → Shift → Improve**; sequence matters because of marginal cost of avoided CO₂.
- **15-Minute City** = Carlos Moreno, 2016; walk catchment = 1.25 km, cycle catchment = 3.75 km for a 15-min trip.
- **TOD** = Calthorpe, 1993; 500–800 m walkshed, FAR 2.5–4.5 in core, reduces car use by 20–40%.
- **MaaS** = Leviä (Helsinki, 2014) — single digital platform for multi-modal journey planning & payment.
- **ITS** = ISO 14813-1:2015; subsystems ATCS, AVL, AFC, C-ITS.
- **pphpd** = passengers per hour per direction — *always* include the "per direction" suffix.
- **Modal share formula** $M_i = (T_i / T_{total}) \times 100$ — sums to 100% (sanity check).
- **CO₂ saved** by modal shift = $\Delta f \times N \times d_{total}$; typical $f$ values — Car 150, Bus 70, BRT 60, Metro 25–40, Cycle/Walk 0 gCO₂/pkm.
- **BCR** = $\sum B/(1+r)^t \div \sum C/(1+r)^t$; BCR > 1 = economically sustainable; annualise to project life T.
- **Headway capacity** $C = 60 C_v / h$; example 6-coach × 240 pax at 3-min headway = 28 800 pphpd.
- **India PM2.5 NAAQS** = 40 µg/m³ annual; CPCB 2009 (revised).
- **Equity** is non-optional — gender-sensitive, disability-friendly, low-income-inclusive design is part of the KTU 2024 NEP expectation.
- **EVs alone ≠ sustainable** — must be paired with grid decarbonisation, charging infrastructure, and a shift from private cars to shared PT.
- **Hierarchy of modes** in a sustainable city = **Walk > Cycle > PT (BRT/Metro) > Shared EV > Private EV > ICE private car** (the *inverse-priority* design).
- **Indicative Indian city modal split:** NMT 25–40%, PT 15–35%, IPT/2W 30–40%, Car 10–25%.
- **Cities for case studies (memorise at least 2):** Kochi (water-metro + metro), Surat (BRT), Bengaluru (Metro + suburban rail), Indore (BRT — best India example), Delhi (metro), London (congestion charge), Singapore (ERP), Paris (15-min city), Copenhagen (cycling).

> **End of Module 3 — Sustainable Urban Mobility Solutions**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrated Mobility Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🚆 MODULE 3: INTEGRATED MOBILITY SYSTEMS (IMS)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Mobility Systems (IMS)** is a holistic, user-centric paradigm of transportation planning and management that seamlessly interconnects diverse modes of transport — public transit, shared mobility, active transport (walking/cycling), electric vehicles, and intelligent transport technologies — through unified digital platforms, interoperable payment systems, coordinated infrastructure, and harmonized policy frameworks, to deliver **safe, equitable, efficient, and environmentally sustainable** movement of people and goods.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, IMS is examined through the lens of:
- **Ethical responsibility** to provide inclusive mobility for all socioeconomic strata.
- **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** — particularly **SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure)**, **SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities)**, and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.
- **Engineering ethics** in designing systems that prioritize human safety, accessibility, and environmental stewardship.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Integrated Mobility Systems like the human circulatory system.**
>
> Just as blood flows seamlessly between the heart (major hub), arteries (express routes), capillaries (local streets), and veins (return paths) — all interconnected and serving different functions — an IMS ensures a commuter can move from a bicycle (capillary) → to a metro (artery) → to a feeder bus (capillary) using a **single ticket, one app, and zero friction**.
>
> Before IMS, transportation was like having isolated organs that don't talk to each other. IMS makes the entire city "breathe" as one connected organism.

### 🎯 Core Objectives of IMS

| **S.No** | **Objective** | **Engineering Ethics Link** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Seamless modal integration | Justice & equity in access |
| 2 | Reduction of carbon footprint | Responsibility to environment |
| 3 | Universal accessibility for disabled/elderly | Non-discrimination |
| 4 | Real-time data-driven optimization | Transparency & accountability |
| 5 | Economic viability for operators & users | Fairness in cost-sharing |
| 6 | Safety across all modes | Non-maleficence (do no harm) |

### 📌 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Standard Metrics)

- **MaaS (Mobility as a Service)** — A subscription-based integrated mobility service model where users pay for transport as a unified service bundle.
- **ITS (Intelligent Transportation Systems)** — Application of **ICT (Information and Communication Technology)**, sensors, and AI to improve transport efficiency.
- **Modal Share** — The percentage of total trips made using a particular transport mode.
- **Multimodal Transport** — Use of two or more modes of transport in a single journey.
- **Last-Mile Connectivity** — The final leg of a journey connecting a major transit hub to the final destination.
- **PTV (Public Transport Vehicle)** — Standard mass-transit metric unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **PMN (Passenger-Mile Network)** index and **VKT (Vehicle Kilometers Travelled)** are globally accepted measures of IMS efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Integrated Mobility Systems

The architecture of any IMS rests on **six interdependent pillars**. Mastery of these is critical for KTU University Examinations.

### 🔷 Pillar 1: Physical Infrastructure Integration

This refers to the **hardware layer** — physical assets that enable modal transfer.

- **Intermodal Hubs** (e.g., Kochi Metro's multi-modal hub at Vyttila Junction integrating metro, bus, ferry, and autorickshaw).
- **Dedicated corridors** for BRT (Bus Rapid Transit), cycling tracks, and pedestrian zones.
- **Park-and-Ride facilities** at suburban transit nodes.
- **Universal design features**: tactile paving, ramps, audio signals for the visually impaired.

> The "Why": Without physical integration, the system is just a collection of disconnected modes — defeating the entire IMS philosophy.

### 🔷 Pillar 2: Digital & Technological Integration

- **Integrated Ticketing Systems** — Single smart card / QR-based access across buses, metro, ferries, parking.
- **Real-time Passenger Information Systems (PIS)** — Live ETAs, crowd density, route replanning.
- **MaaS Apps** — Unified platforms (e.g., Whim in Helsinki, Moovit globally).
- **IoT Sensors & Big Data Analytics** — Dynamic fleet management, predictive maintenance.
- **AI-driven route optimization** and **autonomous shuttle integration**.

### 🔷 Pillar 3: Institutional & Policy Integration

- **Unified Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs)** — A single body governing all modes within a city.
- **National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP, India, 2006 / revised 2014)**.
- **FAME India Scheme (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles)**.
- **Smart City Mission** convergence with IMS.
- **Ethical governance**: Anti-corruption, public participation in planning, RTI (Right to Information) compliance.

### 🔷 Pillar 4: Operational Integration

- **Synchronized timetables** — Trains/buses timed to minimize transfer wait.
- **Common scheduling platforms** for operators.
- **Co-located terminals** (e.g., railway station + metro station + bus bay within 200m).
- **Coordinated fare structures** — Zonal/regional passes.

### 🔷 Pillar 5: Environmental & Sustainability Integration

- **Modal shift from private ICE vehicles to public/shared/active transport** — the single largest carbon mitigation lever in urban transport.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) fleets** for buses, autos, and shared mobility.
- **Non-motorized transport (NMT) infrastructure** — protected cycle tracks, pedestrian plazas.
- **Green corridor design** — preserving biodiversity buffers along transit routes.
- **Renewable energy** powering transit infrastructure (solar-powered metro stations, e.g., Kochi Metro's solar plant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sustainability Metric:** The **Carbon Intensity (CI)** of a transport system is measured in **grams of CO₂-equivalent per passenger-kilometer (gCO₂e/pkm)**.
> - **Private Car:** ~150–200 gCO₂e/pkm
> - **Bus:** ~60–100 gCO₂e/pkm
> - **Metro (electric grid):** ~20–40 gCO₂e/pkm
> - **Cycle:** ~0 gCO₂e/pkm (direct emissions)
> - **Walking:** 0 gCO₂e/pkm (direct emissions)

### 🔷 Pillar 6: Social & Ethical Integration

- **Gender-sensitive design** — Separate coaches, well-lit footpaths, harassment-reporting mechanisms (e.g., She-Bus in Kerala, 2017).
- **Affordability** — Subsidized fares for low-income groups, students, senior citizens.
- **Disability inclusion** — Compliant with **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (India)**.
- **Rural-urban equity** — Extending IMS principles to Tier-2/3 cities and rural feeder routes.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Analytical Framework: The IMS Maturity Model

| **Level** | **Stage** | **Characteristics** | **Indian Example** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | **Fragmented** | Independent modes, no coordination | Most Indian cities pre-2005 |
| **2** | **Coordinated** | Some timetable alignment, common tickets emerging | Delhi (DMRC + DTC beginning) |
| **3** | **Integrated** | Unified authority, MaaS platforms, intermodal hubs | Kochi (Kerala), Bengaluru (Namma Metro + BMTC) |
| **4** | **Smart & Sustainable** | AI-driven, EV-dominant, NMT-first, carbon-neutral | Singapore, Copenhagen (global benchmarks) |
| **5** | **Autonomous & Equitable** | Fully autonomous fleets, zero-emission, universal access | Aspirational / Future state |

> [!NOTE]
> **India's Position (as of 2024):** Most Indian metros are between **Level 2 and Level 3**. Globally, cities like Helsinki (Finland) and Singapore are at **Level 4**.

---

## 2.3 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Applied to IMS

The **Triple Bottom Line framework** (Elkington, 1994) is the standard ethical evaluation lens for IMS in KTU 2024 examinations.

$$
TBL_{IMS} = f(\text{Social Equity}, \text{Environmental Stewardship}, \text{Economic Viability})
$$

| **Dimension** | **Key Indicators** | **Engineering Ethics Principle** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Social (People)** | Accessibility index, fare affordability, safety statistics, gender inclusion | Justice, Beneficence |
| **Environmental (Planet)** | gCO₂e/pkm, air quality (PM2.5), energy mix (renewable %), modal share | Non-maleficence, Sustainability |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Farebox recovery ratio, cost per pkm, ridership, public investment ROI | Accountability, Efficiency |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated analytical toolkit for IMS-related numerical/analytical questions in KTU University Examinations.

| **#** | **Metric / Formula** | **Symbol & Unit** | **Application in IMS** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Modal Share: $MS_i = \dfrac{T_i}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | $MS_i$ = % share of mode $i$; $T_i$ = trips via mode $i$ | Measures shift from private to public modes |
| 2 | Carbon Intensity: $CI = \dfrac{CO_2 \, (g)}{pkm}$ | gCO₂e/pkm | Compares environmental performance of modes |
| 3 | Farebox Recovery Ratio: $FRR = \dfrac{\text{Fare Revenue}}{\text{Operating Cost}} \times 100$ | % | Economic sustainability of public transit |
| 4 | Accessibility Index: $AI = \sum_{j} O_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Equity measurement (jobs reachable in 30 min) |
| 5 | Level of Service (LOS): $LOS = \dfrac{V}{C}$ | ratio (A–F scale) | Traffic flow quality at hub |
| 6 | Reduction in emissions from modal shift: $\Delta CO_2 = T_{shift} \times (CI_{car} - CI_{transit})$ | gCO₂e/day | Quantifies IMS environmental benefit |
| 7 | Cost-Benefit Ratio: $CBR = \dfrac{\sum B_t / (1+r)^t}{\sum C_t / (1+r)^t}$ | dimensionless | Justifies public investment in IMS |
| 8 | Energy Efficiency: $EE = \dfrac{pkm}{MJ}$ | pkm/MJ | Compares energy intensity of modes |
| 9 | Trip Generation (basic): $T = f(D, I, S, ...)$ | trips/day | Forecasts IMS demand |
| 10 | Safety Index: $SI = \dfrac{\text{Fatalities}}{10^5 \text{ pkm}}$ | fatalities per 100M pkm | Ethical imperative — non-maleficence |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Protocol Reminder:** In exam answer sheets, when writing the above table, NEVER use unescaped vertical bars $\vert$ inside cells. Use the words "absolute value of" or the LaTeX `\vert` command.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use IMS frameworks to design **Transit-Oriented Development (TOD)** zones.
- **Civil Engineers** integrate IMS principles into highway design, drainage, and structural loading for multi-modal corridors.
- **Electrical Engineers** design EV charging infrastructure and smart-grid integration for metro systems.
- **Computer Science Engineers** build MaaS platforms, real-time routing algorithms, and digital ticketing systems.
- **Mechanical Engineers** design efficient, low-emission transit vehicle fleets (including electric and hydrogen-powered buses).

> IMS is fundamentally **interdisciplinary** — it is one of the most powerful illustrations of how engineering ethics and sustainable development converge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Quantifying the Sustainability Impact of a Modal Shift

> **KTU-Type Problem (Analytical — Part B Standard):**
> *"A city has 1,000,000 daily motorized trips, with 70% by private car and 30% by bus. As part of an Integrated Mobility System initiative, the city plans to shift 30% of car trips to metro rail. Calculate the daily reduction in CO₂ emissions, given:*
> *- CI (car) = 170 gCO₂e/pkm*
> *- CI (bus) = 80 gCO₂e/pkm*
> *- CI (metro) = 30 gCO₂e/pkm*
> *- Average trip length = 12 km"*

### 📐 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the initial modal share (Baseline)**

$$
T_{total} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{car}^{initial} = 0.70 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 700{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{bus}^{initial} = 0.30 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 300{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{metro}^{initial} = 0 \text{ trips/day}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the modal shift (30% of car trips → metro)**

The problem states that 30% of car trips shift to metro.

$$
T_{shift} = 0.30 \times 700{,}000 = 210{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** "30% of car trips" means 30% of the existing 700,000 car trips, NOT 30% of total trips. The wording is critical in KTU valuation.

**Step 3: Compute new modal distribution**

$$
T_{car}^{new} = 700{,}000 - 210{,}000 = 490{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{metro}^{new} = 0 + 210{,}000 = 210{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{bus}^{new} = 300{,}000 \text{ trips/day (unchanged)}
$$

**Step 4: Compute baseline daily CO₂ emissions**

For each mode, daily emissions = number of trips × trip length × carbon intensity.

$$
E_{car}^{initial} = 700{,}000 \times 12 \text{ km} \times 170 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/pkm}
$$

$$
E_{car}^{initial} = 700{,}000 \times 12 \times 170 = 1{,}428{,}000{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

$$
E_{bus}^{initial} = 300{,}000 \times 12 \times 80 = 288{,}000{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

$$
E_{total}^{initial} = E_{car}^{initial} + E_{bus}^{initial} = 1{,}428{,}000{,}000 + 288{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
E_{total}^{initial} = 1{,}716{,}000{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day} = 1{,}716 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

**Step 5: Compute new daily CO₂ emissions post-IMS intervention**

$$
E_{car}^{new} = 490{,}000 \times 12 \times 170 = 999{,}600{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

$$
E_{metro}^{new} = 210{,}000 \times 12 \times 30 = 75{,}600{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

$$
E_{bus}^{new} = 300{,}000 \times 12 \times 80 = 288{,}000{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

$$
E_{total}^{new} = 999{,}600{,}000 + 75{,}600{,}000 + 288{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
E_{total}^{new} = 1{,}363{,}200{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day} = 1{,}363.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the daily CO₂ reduction**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = E_{total}^{initial} - E_{total}^{new} = 1{,}716{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}363{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 352{,}800{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day} = 352.8 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/day}
$$

**Step 7: Annualize the result for the KTU "engineering ethics & sustainability" framing**

$$
\Delta CO_2^{annual} = 352.8 \times 365 = 128{,}772 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiply by 365 (days/year) to project annual impact — this is what would be reported in a city's Climate Action Plan.

**Step 8: Express as equivalent metrics for ethical communication**

$$
\text{Equivalent cars off-road} = \dfrac{128{,}772}{4.6} \approx 27{,}994 \text{ cars/year}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** One typical passenger car emits ~4.6 tonnes CO₂/year. This makes the abstract number tangible for policy advocacy.

### ✅ Final Answer (Full Marks)

**The IMS-driven modal shift reduces CO₂ emissions by 352.8 tonnes/day, or approximately 1,28,772 tonnes/year — equivalent to removing nearly 28,000 cars from the road annually.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Scoring Matrix

> **KTU-Type Problem (Conceptual-Analytical — Part A):**
> *"Construct a TBL evaluation matrix for comparing a proposed Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) corridor versus an expansion of private car lanes in a city. Identify 3 indicators per dimension."*

### 📐 Step-by-Step Framework

| **TBL Dimension** | **Indicator** | **BRT Corridor Score (1–5)** | **Private Car Lane Score (1–5)** | **Ethical Reasoning** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Social (People)** | Accessibility for low-income groups | 5 | 1 | BRT serves economically weaker sections equitably (Justice) |
| **Social (People)** | Safety (fatalities per 100M pkm) | 4 | 2 | BRT has lower per-passenger fatality rate (Non-maleficence) |
| **Social (People)** | Gender inclusivity (lighting, frequency) | 5 | 2 | Frequent, well-lit BRT stations reduce harassment risk |
| **Environmental (Planet)** | gCO₂e/pkm | 5 (low) | 1 (high) | BRT drastically lower carbon intensity (Sustainability) |
| **Environmental (Planet)** | Air quality (PM2.5 reduction) | 5 | 1 | Fewer ICE vehicles = better urban air |
| **Environmental (Planet)** | Land use efficiency (pkm/hectare) | 5 | 1 | BRT moves more people in less space |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Cost per pkm (infrastructure) | 4 | 2 | BRT is 10–20× cheaper than metro, more efficient than road expansion |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Farebox recovery potential | 3 | 5 (toll-based) | Car lanes may recover cost via tolls but exclude poor |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Job creation (construction + operation) | 5 | 3 | BRT generates more equitable employment |

### 📊 Aggregate TBL Score

$$
\text{Score}_{BRT} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} S_i^{BRT} = 41/45 \approx 91.1\%
$$

$$
\text{Score}_{CarLane} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} S_i^{Car} = 18/45 = 40.0\%
$$

> **Engineering Ethics Verdict:** The BRT option is decisively superior on the **Triple Bottom Line**, aligning with the **Precautionary Principle** and **Intergenerational Equity** doctrines in engineering ethics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Accessibility Index (Equity Quantification)

> **KTU-Type Problem:**
> *"In two neighborhoods (A and B) of a city, compute the Accessibility Index to jobs using public transport, given 50,000 jobs distributed across 5 zones. Travel time thresholds: 30 min by public transport."*

| **Zone (Jobs)** | **Jobs $O_j$** | **Reachable in 30 min from A?** | **Reachable in 30 min from B?** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10,000 | ✅ Yes (1) | ❌ No (0) |
| 2 | 12,000 | ✅ Yes (1) | ✅ Yes (1) |
| 3 | 8,000 | ❌ No (0) | ❌ No (0) |
| 4 | 15,000 | ✅ Yes (1) | ✅ Yes (1) |
| 5 | 5,000 | ❌ No (0) | ✅ Yes (1) |

**Step 1: Compute Accessibility Index for Neighborhood A**

$$
AI_A = \sum_{j=1}^{5} O_j \cdot f(C_{Aj}) = (10{,}000)(1) + (12{,}000)(1) + (8{,}000)(0) + (15{,}000)(1) + (5{,}000)(0)
$$

$$
AI_A = 10{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 0 + 15{,}000 + 0 = 37{,}000 \text{ jobs reachable}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Accessibility Index for Neighborhood B**

$$
AI_B = (10{,}000)(0) + (12{,}000)(1) + (8{,}000)(0) + (15{,}000)(1) + (5{,}000)(1)
$$

$$
AI_B = 0 + 12{,}000 + 0 + 15{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 32{,}000 \text{ jobs reachable}
$$

**Step 3: Equity Ratio**

$$
\text{Equity Ratio} = \dfrac{\min(AI_A, AI_B)}{\max(AI_A, AI_B)} = \dfrac{32{,}000}{37{,}000} \approx 0.865
$$

> **Ethical Interpretation:** An equity ratio of 0.865 indicates **moderate spatial inequity** — Neighborhood B has 13.5% lower job access. KTU 2024 expects students to **prescribe an IMS intervention** (e.g., new feeder bus, BRTS lane, or NMT corridor) to raise $AI_B$.

---

| **IMS Intervention** | **Primary SDG** | **Secondary SDGs** | **Ethical Principle** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Electric bus fleet | SDG 13 (Climate Action) | SDG 7, SDG 11 | Non-maleficence |
| Metro rail expansion | SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities) | SDG 9, SDG 8 | Justice |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Integrated Mobility Systems (IMS). List any four key components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Integrated Mobility Systems (IMS) is a holistic transportation framework that seamlessly combines multiple modes of transport — public transit, shared mobility, active transport, and intelligent technologies — through unified digital platforms, coordinated infrastructure, and harmonized policies, to deliver safe, equitable, efficient, and environmentally sustainable movement of people and goods.
>
> **Four Key Components (½ mark each, any 4):**
> 1. **Physical Infrastructure Integration** — Intermodal hubs, BRT corridors, NMT networks.
> 2. **Digital Integration** — MaaS platforms, smart ticketing, real-time PIS.
> 3. **Institutional Integration** — Unified Metropolitan Transport Authorities, NUTP.
> 4. **Operational Integration** — Synchronized timetables, common fare systems.
> 5. **Environmental Integration** — EV fleets, renewable energy-powered transit.
> 6. **Social Integration** — Universal accessibility, gender-inclusive design.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**What is Mobility as a Service (MaaS)? How does it contribute to sustainable development?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **MaaS Definition (1.5 marks):** Mobility as a Service (MaaS) is a user-centric, subscription-based integrated mobility model that bundles multiple transport services (public transit, ride-sharing, bike-sharing, taxi, etc.) into a single digital platform, allowing users to plan, book, and pay for end-to-end journeys using one app.
>
> **Contribution to Sustainable Development (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Environmental:** Reduces private vehicle usage → lower CO₂ emissions and air pollution (SDG 13).
> 2. **Social:** Provides equitable, affordable access to mobility for all (SDG 10, SDG 11).
> 3. **Economic:** Optimizes transport resource utilization, reducing congestion costs (SDG 9).
> 4. **Ethical:** Promotes transparency, accountability, and inclusivity in transport access.

---

## 5.2 📚 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Part B Structure:** Each question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **six pillars of Integrated Mobility Systems** with suitable examples from Indian cities. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** in achieving sustainable urban mobility. Highlight at least **three ethical concerns** related to ITS deployment. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q-A (a): Six Pillars of IMS (7 Marks)

| **Pillar** | **Explanation** | **Indian Example** | **Marks** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| **1. Physical Infrastructure** | Intermodal hubs, BRT, NMT, EV charging | Vyttila Hub (Kochi) integrating metro, bus, ferry | 1.5 |
| **2. Digital & Technology** | MaaS, smart cards, real-time PIS | Kochi One Card; Delhi Metro smart card | 1.5 |
| **3. Institutional & Policy** | UMTA, NUTP, FAME India, Smart Cities | Bengaluru Metropolitan Transport Corporation reforms | 1 |
| **4. Operational** | Synchronized timetables, common fares | Delhi Metro + DTC feeder bus coordination | 1 |
| **5. Environmental** | EV buses, solar-powered metro, modal shift | Kochi Metro's solar plant (4.4 MW) | 1 |
| **6. Social & Ethical** | Universal access, gender safety, affordability | She-Bus (Kerala); disabled-friendly Delhi Metro | 1 |

**Concluding Statement (½ mark):** *A successful IMS requires all six pillars to be developed in tandem; weakness in any one pillar compromises the entire system's sustainability and equity.*

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q-A (b): ITS in Sustainable Mobility + Ethical Concerns (7 Marks)

**Role of ITS (4 marks):**
- **Traffic Management:** Adaptive signal control, real-time congestion pricing → reduces idle emissions.
- **Public Transit Enhancement:** Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL), passenger information, predictive maintenance.
- **Safety:** Collision avoidance systems, speed enforcement, emergency response coordination.
- **MaaS Enablement:** ITS provides the data backbone for unified mobility platforms.
- **Example:** Bengaluru's Intelligent Traffic System (ITMS) reduces average commute time by ~12%.

**Three Ethical Concerns (3 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Privacy and Surveillance:** ITS collects massive personal location data. **Ethical violation** if data is shared without informed consent.
2. **Digital Divide:** Smart mobility apps may exclude digitally illiterate, elderly, or low-income populations, deepening inequity.
3. **Algorithmic Bias:** AI-driven routing may deprioritize low-demand (often poorer) neighborhoods, violating **distributive justice**.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):** Students often **list ITS technologies without linking them to sustainable development goals**. Always explicitly connect every ITS function to either **emission reduction, safety improvement, or equity enhancement** to secure full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a **suitable numerical example**, demonstrate how a **modal shift from private cars to public transit** reduces urban carbon emissions. Use the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework to evaluate the shift ethically. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Critically analyze the **engineering ethical challenges** in implementing IMS in **developing economies** like India, with reference to **affordability, accessibility, and accountability**. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q-B (a): Numerical Modal Shift + TBL (7 Marks)

**Numerical Example (3.5 marks):**

> Assume a city has 500,000 daily car trips, average trip length 10 km, CI (car) = 160 gCO₂e/pkm, CI (bus) = 70 gCO₂e/pkm. If 20% of car trips shift to bus:
>
> $$
> T_{shift} = 0.20 \times 500{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ trips/day}
> $$
>
> $$
> \Delta CO_2 = T_{shift} \times L \times (CI_{car} - CI_{bus}) = 100{,}000 \times 10 \times (160 - 70)
> $$
>
> $$
> \Delta CO_2 = 100{,}000 \times 10 \times 90 = 90{,}000{,}000 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/day} = 90 \text{ tonnes/day}
> $$
>
> Annual reduction: $90 \times 365 = 32{,}850$ tonnes CO₂/year.

**TBL Ethical Evaluation (3.5 marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Impact** | **Ethical Principle** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Social** | Improved air quality → reduced respiratory diseases; better last-mile access for poor | Beneficence, Justice |
| **Environmental** | 32,850 tCO₂/year mitigation; lower PM2.5; reduced noise | Sustainability, Non-maleficence |
| **Economic** | Lower fuel import bills; reduced congestion costs (~₹1.5 lakh crore/year nationally); new green jobs | Accountability, Efficiency |

**Concluding Statement (½ mark):** *The modal shift is a clear ethical imperative, but its success depends on reliable, frequent, safe, and affordable public transport — a key engineering challenge.*

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q-B (b): Ethical Challenges in Developing Economies (7 Marks)

| **Ethical Challenge** | **Detailed Discussion** | **Marks** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **1. Affordability** | IMS projects (metro, EV buses) require massive capital investment. In developing economies, fare hikes for cost recovery may exclude the poor. **Conflict:** Economic viability vs. social equity. **Resolution:** Cross-subsidization, life-line tariffs, PPP with public interest safeguards. | 2.5 |
| **2. Accessibility** | Rural-urban digital divide, disability exclusion, gender gap in mobility. IMS design often defaults to "average user," neglecting marginalized groups. **Ethical principle violated:** Justice, Non-discrimination. **Resolution:** Universal design, gender audits, last-mile rural connectivity. | 2.5 |
| **3. Accountability** | Multiple agencies (rail, road, metro, municipal) → unclear accountability. Corruption in land acquisition, contractor selection, fare-setting undermines public trust. **Ethical principle violated:** Transparency, Accountability. **Resolution:** RTI, UMTA, public participation, ombudsman. | 2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):** A common mistake is **discussing IMS challenges in generic terms** without **anchoring them to engineering ethics principles** (justice, beneficence, non-maleficence, sustainability, accountability). KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **ethical reasoning ability** — every challenge must be tied to a named principle to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 KTU Examiner's Quick-Recall Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision Points for IMS (Module 3):**

- ✅ **IMS = Holistic integration** of 6 pillars: Physical, Digital, Institutional, Operational, Environmental, Social.
- ✅ **MaaS** is the digital face of IMS — single app, single payment, multiple modes.
- ✅ **Modal shift to public/NMT transport is the #1 carbon mitigation lever** in urban transport.
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — People, Planet, Profit — is the ethical evaluation framework.
- ✅ **Carbon Intensity benchmarks:** Car ~170 > Bus ~80 > Metro ~30 > Cycle ~0 gCO₂e/pkm.
- ✅ **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR)** measures economic sustainability of transit.
- ✅ **Accessibility Index (AI)** quantifies equity — used to identify underserved neighborhoods.
- ✅ **IMS Maturity:** 5 levels — Fragmented → Coordinated → Integrated → Smart/Sustainable → Autonomous/Equitable.
- ✅ **SDG linkages:** SDG 9 (Infrastructure), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities), SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 10 (Inequality Reduction), SDG 3 (Health).
- ✅ **Indian policy framework:** NUTP 2006/2014, FAME India, Smart City Mission, Metro Rail Policy 2017.
- ✅ **Ethical principles to cite in every answer:** Justice, Non-maleficence, Beneficence, Sustainability, Accountability, Transparency, Intergenerational Equity.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:** Modal Share, Carbon Intensity, FRR, Accessibility Index, Cost-Benefit Ratio.
- ✅ **Famous case study hooks:** Kochi Metro (India's first solar-powered metro), Delhi Metro (size and ridership), Singapore (Level 4 global benchmark), Helsinki (Whim MaaS pioneer), Copenhagen (cycling integration model).
- ✅ **For numerical problems:** Always show unit conversions explicitly (g → tonnes, daily → annual) — KTU examiners award step-marks for clean transitions.
- ✅ **In Part B answers:** Always conclude with an **ethical verdict** explicitly stating which engineering ethics principle is satisfied or violated.

> [!NOTE]
> **🎓 Final Tip from KTU Board Perspective:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **integration of ethics + sustainability + technical concepts**. Students who answer *only technically* (without ethics) or *only ethically* (without technical depth) will lose 30–40% of marks. The sweet spot is **blending both seamlessly in every answer**.

---

topics.title: E-Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** E-Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **E-Mobility (Electric Mobility)** refers to the integrated ecosystem of transportation systems, technologies, infrastructure, and policies that enable the movement of people and goods using **electrically powered drivetrains** — encompassing Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), and Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — supported by renewable energy generation, smart charging infrastructure, and sustainable end-of-life battery management.

In the context of **Engineering Ethics and Sustainable Development**, E-Mobility is treated not merely as a propulsion technology, but as a **socio-technical transition** that addresses the triple bottom line: **environmental protection** (decarbonization), **social equity** (clean air, accessibility), and **economic viability** (energy security, new industries).

> [!NOTE] **Key Sustainability Metric (IEA Standard)**
> An EV becomes a true sustainable mobility solution only when its **lifecycle CO₂ emissions** drop below those of an equivalent Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle. Globally accepted threshold: **~20,000 – 30,000 km of driving** before an EV "breaks even" with an ICE car in lifetime carbon footprint, provided the electricity grid is moderately decarbonized.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **smartphone**. You charge it at night, use it all day, and plug it back in. The battery is the heart of the system — store energy when cheap/clean, use it when needed.

**E-Mobility applies this exact same logic to cars, buses, and trucks:**

- **The Battery** = the phone's battery, but 100–1000× larger.
- **The Electric Motor** = replaces the petrol/diesel engine (no gears needed in many designs).
- **The Charger** = the phone's wall plug, but high-power (up to **350 kW DC fast chargers**).
- **The Grid** = the wall socket, ideally powered by **solar / wind / hydro**.

So just as your smartphone replaced the landline (cleaner, smarter, modular), **E-Mobility is replacing the gasoline car with a cleaner, smarter, electrified transport system** — and critically, the electricity can come from sunlight, not from drilled crude oil.

> [!TIP] **KTU Board Hint**
> Examiners love questions framed around the **"Petrol Pump → Charging Station"** analogy. Memorize that the shift is not just "fuel change" but an **energy vector change**: from chemical (petroleum) to electrical (electron flow).

### 1.3 Core Pillars of E-Mobility (Syllabus-Mandated)

| Pillar | Description | Ethical / Sustainable Link |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Electrification** | Replacing ICE with electric drivetrain | Direct tail-pipe emission elimination |
| **Clean Energy Supply** | Powering EVs with renewables | Decarbonization of transport sector |
| **Charging Infrastructure** | Public/private charging networks | Equitable access (urban + rural) |
| **Battery Lifecycle Management** | Mining → Use → Recycling | Circular economy, ethical sourcing |
| **Policy & Regulation** | FAME-II, EV Policy 2025, BS-VII norms | Government & industry ethics |
| **Consumer Awareness** | Range anxiety, total cost of ownership | Transparency in marketing |

---

### 1.4 Explicit Standard Metrics (Board-Exam Relevant)

- **Energy consumption unit:** **kWh / 100 km** (the "MPGe equivalent" for EVs)
- **Battery capacity unit:** **kWh**
- **Range unit:** **km on a single full charge**
- **Charging power:** **kW** (AC: 3.3 – 22 kW; DC Fast: 50 – 350 kW)
- **Battery State of Charge (SoC):** **% from 0 → 100**
- **C-rate:** Charging/discharging speed relative to battery capacity
- **CO₂ savings benchmark:** **~1.5 – 2.5 tonnes CO₂/year** per EV (vs. ICE equivalent)

---

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake**
> E-Mobility is **NOT the same as "Electric Vehicles" alone**. It is a **system** — it includes renewable generation, grid integration, end-of-life recycling, and policy. Writing only about vehicles in a 14-mark answer will fetch partial credit at best.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Architectural Classes of Electrified Vehicles

Understanding the **taxonomy** of electric vehicles is foundational. Examiners frequently test the differences via **comparison tables** or **"justify why"** questions.

#### A) **BEV — Battery Electric Vehicle (Pure Electric)**
- **Energy source:** Grid electricity only (100% electric).
- **No combustion engine.** No petrol tank.
- Examples: Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3, BYD Atto 3.
- **Zero tailpipe emissions.** Real-world energy use: **15 – 20 kWh / 100 km**.

#### B) **PHEV — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle**
- **Dual powertrain:** Electric motor + ICE engine.
- Battery: **8 – 18 kWh**, charges from grid.
- Pure-electric range: **30 – 60 km**, then switches to hybrid mode.
- Examples: Toyota Prius Prime, Volvo XC90 T8, BMW 330e.
- **Use case:** Transition technology for range-anxious users.

#### C) **HEV — Hybrid Electric Vehicle (Self-Charging)**
- **Cannot be plugged in.** Battery recharged via regenerative braking + ICE.
- Small battery: **1 – 2 kWh**.
- Examples: Toyota Camry Hybrid, Honda City e:HEV.
- **Ethical note:** Marketing "self-charging hybrid" is considered **greenwashing** by some consumer bodies — KTU ethics perspective.

#### D) **FCEV — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle**
- **Hydrogen as fuel.** Fuel cell generates electricity onboard via reaction with O₂.
- **Only emission:** Pure water vapor.
- Examples: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
- **Limitation:** Hydrogen production, storage, and refueling infrastructure challenges.

### 2.2 Core Subsystems of an Electric Powertrain (Engineering Layer)

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem** — Li-ion / LFP / Solid-state battery pack.
2. **Power Conversion Subsystem** — Inverter (DC → AC) for motor drive.
3. **Electric Traction Motor** — PMSM, Induction, or SRM (typical efficiency **90 – 95%**).
4. **Transmission** — Often single-speed reduction gearbox.
5. **Onboard Charger (OBC)** — AC → DC for slow charging.
6. **Thermal Management** — Liquid cooling for battery + motor.
7. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — SoC, SoH, cell balancing, safety.
8. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — The "brain" coordinating torque, regen, and energy flow.
9. **Regenerative Braking** — Recovers kinetic energy back to battery (typically **10 – 30%** energy recovery).
10. **DC Fast-Charge Port** — CCS2 / CHAdeMO / Type-2 connectors (India standard: **CCS2**).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these formulas — they appear in numerical sub-parts.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy stored in battery** | $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | kWh | Battery capacity × voltage |
| 2 | **Energy per 100 km (consumption)** | $E_c = \dfrac{E_{bat} \times 100}{R_{range}}$ | kWh / 100 km | EV efficiency metric |
| 3 | **Vehicle range (theoretical)** | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_c} \times 100$ | km | Distance on full charge |
| 4 | **Charging time** | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{delivered}}{P_{charger} \times \eta_{ch}}$ | hours | Time to add kWh of energy |
| 5 | **C-rate (charging/discharging)** | $C = \dfrac{I}{C_{Ah}}$ | dimensionless | Current relative to capacity |
| 6 | **Regenerative energy recovered** | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \times \eta_{regen}$ | Wh | Kinetic energy → electrical |
| 7 | **CO₂ equivalent emissions** | $CO_2 = E_{use} \times EF_{grid}$ | kg CO₂ | Grid emission factor × energy |
| 8 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{buy} + C_{fuel/elec} + C_{maint} - C_{residual}$ | ₹ / lifetime | Real economic comparison |
| 9 | **Motor power requirement** | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$ | kW | Force × velocity / efficiency |
| 10 | **CO₂ savings vs ICE** | $\Delta CO_2 = CO_2^{ICE} - CO_2^{EV}$ | kg CO₂ / year | Sustainability benefit |
| 11 | **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum C_t / (1+r)^t}{\sum E_t / (1+r)^t}$ | ₹ / kWh | Lifetime unit cost of electricity |
| 12 | **Charge delivered (SoC change)** | $\Delta E = (SoC_{final} - SoC_{initial}) \times E_{bat}$ | kWh | Energy added during a session |
| 13 | **State of Health (degradation)** | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{current}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Battery health metric |
| 14 | **Specific energy of battery** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{m_{bat}}$ | Wh / kg | Energy per unit mass |
| 15 | **Energy density of battery** | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V_{bat}}$ | Wh / L | Energy per unit volume |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the careful LaTeX spacing: in formulas, the use of `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) avoids breaking the markdown table syntax where applicable, and every cell uses **bold** for column headers per KTU examiner presentation standards.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Concepts

- **Why is EV efficiency higher than ICE?** Because electric motors convert **> 90% of grid energy to motion**, while ICE vehicles waste **~70%** as heat (2nd Law of Thermodynamics). A typical ICE: 20 – 35% thermal efficiency; a typical EV drivetrain: **85 – 95%**.
- **Why does the battery get hot during fast charging?** Higher C-rate means higher $I^2R$ (Joule heating) losses, plus electrochemical heat from lithium intercalation. Hence **liquid cooling** is mandatory above ~50 kW DC charging.
- **Why is regenerative braking limited to 10–30%?** Because the battery's maximum charge acceptance rate, friction-brake blending safety, and stability during cornering all impose engineering constraints.
- **Why is FCEV emission "only water" technically correct but ethically incomplete?** Because the upstream **hydrogen production** (95% globally is still from fossil "grey hydrogen") carries significant CO₂. This is a classic KTU ethics question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Smart grids** use EVs as **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** storage — your parked car stabilizes the grid during peak demand.
- **Renewable integration:** EVs absorb midday solar surplus that would otherwise be curtailed.
- **Public transport electrification:** India's **FAME-II** and **PM e-Bus Sewa** schemes aim for **50,000+ e-buses**.
- **Logistics:** Amazon, Flipkart, and Zomato are piloting **electric last-mile delivery fleets** in Kerala and Bengaluru.
- **Heavy-duty trucks:** Tesla Semi, BharatBenz, and Tata are working on long-haul electric trucks with **600+ kWh** packs.

---

> [!TIP] **Board Exam Tip**
> Always link formulas to **sustainability outcomes** in your answer. E.g., "Using $E = V \times C$, a 60 kWh battery in a Tata Nexon EV delivers ~312 km range, displacing ~1.8 tonnes of CO₂/year compared to a 1.5 L ICE car on India's grid emission factor of **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**."

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Battery Energy and Range Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> An electric car uses a **400 V nominal** battery pack with a capacity of **150 Ah**. The vehicle consumes **18 kWh per 100 km**. Calculate:
> (a) Total energy stored in the battery in kWh.
> (b) Theoretical range on a full charge.
> (c) Time to charge from 20% to 80% SoC using a 50 kW DC fast charger (assume 92% charging efficiency).

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Energy stored in battery:**

We use the relation $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$, then convert Wh → kWh.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bat} &= V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} \\
&= 400 \text{ V} \times 150 \text{ Ah} \\
&= 60{,}000 \text{ Wh} \\
&= 60 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ Wh}$, so divide by 1000.

**(b) Theoretical range:**

We rearrange the range formula $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_c} \times 100$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_c} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{60 \text{ kWh}}{18 \text{ kWh/100 km}} \\
&= \dfrac{60}{18} \times 100 \\
&= 3.333 \times 100 \\
&= 333.3 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Energy per 100 km means we are asking "how many groups of 100 km fit into the total range." Direct division then multiplied by 100 gives the actual range.

**(c) Charging time from 20% to 80% SoC:**

Step 1 — Energy to be delivered ($\Delta E$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= (SoC_{final} - SoC_{initial}) \times E_{bat} \\
&= (0.80 - 0.20) \times 60 \text{ kWh} \\
&= 0.60 \times 60 \\
&= 36 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Effective charging power at the battery terminal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eff} &= P_{charger} \times \eta_{ch} \\
&= 50 \text{ kW} \times 0.92 \\
&= 46 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Charging time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ch} &= \dfrac{\Delta E}{P_{eff}} \\
&= \dfrac{36 \text{ kWh}}{46 \text{ kW}} \\
&= 0.7826 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 47 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A kWh divided by a kW leaves hours, the standard unit of time. Multiply by 60 to convert hours → minutes for a more intuitive answer.

✅ **Final Answers:**
- (a) $E_{bat} = 60 \text{ kWh}$
- (b) $R = 333.3 \text{ km}$
- (c) $t_{ch} \approx 47 \text{ minutes}$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — CO₂ Savings vs. ICE (Sustainability Analysis)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A conventional petrol car consumes **6 L / 100 km** and is driven **15,000 km/year**. An equivalent EV consumes **15 kWh / 100 km**. Given:
> - Petrol emission factor: **2.31 kg CO₂ / L**
> - Indian grid emission factor: **0.72 kg CO₂ / kWh**
> Calculate the **annual CO₂ savings** of switching to the EV.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Annual fuel/energy consumption:**

Petrol car:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{petrol} &= 6 \text{ L/100 km} \times 15{,}000 \text{ km/year} \\
&= 900 \text{ L/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

EV:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{EV} &= 15 \text{ kWh/100 km} \times 15{,}000 \text{ km/year} \\
&= 2{,}250 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual CO₂ emissions from petrol car:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{ICE} &= V_{petrol} \times EF_{petrol} \\
&= 900 \times 2.31 \\
&= 2{,}079 \text{ kg CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual CO₂ emissions from EV (well-to-wheel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{EV} &= E_{EV} \times EF_{grid} \\
&= 2{,}250 \times 0.72 \\
&= 1{,}620 \text{ kg CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual CO₂ savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2 &= CO_2^{ICE} - CO_2^{EV} \\
&= 2{,}079 - 1{,}620 \\
&= 459 \text{ kg CO₂/year} \\
&\approx 0.46 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Lifetime savings (assume 10-year ownership):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2^{lifetime} &= 459 \times 10 \\
&= 4{,}590 \text{ kg} \\
&= 4.59 \text{ tonnes CO₂}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent forest area needed to absorb this CO₂:**
A typical tree absorbs ~**22 kg CO₂/year**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trees} &= \dfrac{\Delta CO_2^{lifetime}}{22 \times 10} \\
&= \dfrac{4{,}590}{220} \\
&\approx 21 \text{ mature trees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This last step is the ethical "human scale" comparison that examiners reward — translating tonnes of CO₂ into a tangible quantity (trees) the reader can picture.

✅ **Final Answer:** The EV saves **459 kg CO₂/year**, equivalent to **~21 trees** absorbing for a decade.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Charging Power and C-Rate

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 50 kWh EV battery is charged at **120 A** constant current. What is the **C-rate**? If the rated capacity is **120 Ah**, how long does a full charge take (theoretical, ignoring taper)?

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — C-rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \text{ A}}{120 \text{ Ah}} \\
&= 1 \text{ C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A C-rate of 1C means the battery would be fully charged (or discharged) in exactly 1 hour at this current.

**Step 2 — Full charge time at 1C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{C_{rated}}{I} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \text{ Ah}}{120 \text{ A}} \\
&= 1 \text{ hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Final Answer:** 1C rate, **1 hour** theoretical full-charge time.

> [!NOTE]
> In real fast charging, the current is **not constant** — chargers use **CC-CV (Constant Current → Constant Voltage)** profile. The current tapers off as SoC approaches 100% to protect cell longevity. This nuance earns extra marks in 14-mark answers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | ICE Vehicle | BEV (Battery EV) | PHEV | FCEV (Hydrogen) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Petrol/Diesel/CNG | Grid electricity | Grid + Petrol | Hydrogen (H₂) |
| **Tailpipe emissions** | CO₂, CO, NOₓ, PM | **Zero** | Zero (electric mode) | Zero (only H₂O) |
| **Well-to-wheel efficiency** | 20 – 35% | 60 – 80% | 35 – 50% | 25 – 35% (currently) |
| **Refuel/charge time** | 3 – 5 min | 30 min – 8 h | 3 – 5 h (AC) / 30 min (DC) | 5 – 10 min |
| **Range (typical)** | 500 – 800 km | 200 – 600 km | 500 – 800 km | 400 – 650 km |
| **Operating cost (₹/km)** | ₹6 – 9 | ₹0.8 – 1.5 | ₹3 – 5 | ₹5 – 8 (projected) |
| **Maintenance** | High (oil, filters) | **Very low** | Moderate | Low (no oil) |
| **Key challenge** | Emissions, oil import | Range anxiety, charging | Cost, complexity | H₂ infrastructure |
| **Sustainability rating** | Low | High (with clean grid) | Medium | Medium (depends on H₂ source) |

---

> [!TIP] **Examiner's Insight**
> The **"well-to-wheel"** concept is the heart of E-Mobility ethics. An EV is only as green as its electricity source. In Kerala, with ~70% of electricity from solar + hydro + thermal mix, EVs already deliver substantial CO₂ savings. In coal-heavy states, the savings are smaller but still positive.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark conceptual / 1-mark application.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define E-Mobility. List any four types of electric vehicles with one key feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **E-Mobility** is the integrated ecosystem of vehicles, infrastructure, energy sources, and policies that enable transport using electrical drivetrains, with the goal of achieving sustainable, low-carbon mobility.

The four main types are:

1. **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle):** Powered solely by grid-charged batteries; zero tailpipe emissions.
2. **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Combines an electric motor with an ICE engine; can be plugged in for 30–60 km of pure-electric range.
3. **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Cannot be plugged in; recharges via regenerative braking and the ICE; small battery (1–2 kWh).
4. **FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle):** Uses hydrogen to generate electricity onboard; only emits water vapor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is "well-to-wheel" efficiency? Compare it for an ICE car and a BEV.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Well-to-wheel (WTW) efficiency** is the cumulative energy efficiency measured from the **primary energy source** (well = oil well, coal mine, sun) to the **wheels of the vehicle** delivering useful motion. It accounts for extraction, refining/conversion, transport, and final drivetrain losses.

**Comparison:**

- **ICE car:** Crude oil extraction → refining → transport → engine combustion (~30% thermal efficiency) → drivetrain (~85% mechanical). Overall WTW efficiency ≈ **20 – 25%**.
- **BEV:** Power plant (coal/solar/wind) → grid transmission (~92%) → battery charging (~90%) → motor (~92%) → wheels (~95%). Overall WTW efficiency ≈ **60 – 80%** (with clean grid, up to ~85%).

Hence, even when powered by coal electricity, a BEV is **2–3× more efficient** than a petrol car in converting primary energy to motion, making it a more sustainable choice.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **major components of an Electric Vehicle powertrain** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **Battery Management System (BMS)** and **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A four-wheeler EV has a **battery pack of 60 kWh**, energy consumption of **16 kWh / 100 km**, and supports **50 kW DC fast charging** with **90% charging efficiency**. Calculate:
1. The **theoretical full-charge range**.
2. The **time to charge from 10% to 80% SoC**.
3. The **annual CO₂ savings** if the car is driven 12,000 km/year, replacing a petrol car with mileage of 15 km/L. Use emission factors: petrol = 2.31 kg CO₂/L, Indian grid = 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh.

---

**Model Answer (a) — EV Powertrain Components:**

The major components of an EV powertrain are:

1. **Battery Pack** — Stores electrical energy. Modern EVs use **Lithium-ion** cells (NMC, LFP, or NCA chemistries). Voltage ranges from **400 V (standard) to 800 V (premium, e.g., Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan)**.

2. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — The "guardian" of the battery. It performs:
   - **State of Charge (SoC) estimation** — like a fuel gauge.
   - **State of Health (SoH) tracking** — battery degradation over years.
   - **Cell balancing** — equalizing voltage across hundreds of cells.
   - **Thermal monitoring** — preventing overheating and thermal runaway.
   - **Safety cutoffs** — isolating the pack during crash or short-circuit.

3. **Inverter (DC → AC Converter)** — Converts the battery's DC to 3-phase AC for the traction motor. Uses **IGBT or SiC MOSFET** switches for high efficiency (>97%).

4. **Electric Traction Motor** — Typically **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** or **Induction Motor**. Delivers instant peak torque (e.g., 250 Nm from 0 rpm), enabling rapid acceleration.

5. **Onboard Charger (OBC)** — Converts AC grid supply to DC for slow/home charging (3.3 – 22 kW).

6. **DC Fast-Charge Port (CCS2 in India)** — Bypasses the OBC; charges the battery directly at 50 – 350 kW.

7. **Reduction Gearbox** — Single-speed transmission; electric motors have wide RPM range, eliminating multi-gear complexity.

8. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — The "brain" of the EV. It coordinates:
   - Accelerator pedal input → motor torque command.
   - Brake pedal → blending friction brakes with regenerative braking.
   - BMS data → thermal management decisions.
   - Drive mode selection (Eco / Normal / Sport).
   - V2G energy dispatch when parked.

9. **Thermal Management System** — Liquid cooling loop for battery, inverter, and motor to maintain optimal operating temperature (~25 – 35°C).

10. **Regenerative Braking System** — Converts kinetic energy during deceleration back to electrical energy. Typical recovery: **10 – 30%** of total energy used.

**Block Diagram:**

```
GRID → [CCS2 Port] → [OBC] → [Battery Pack] ⇄ [BMS]
                                      ↓
                                  [Inverter]
                                      ↓
                              [Traction Motor] → [Gearbox] → [Wheels]
                                      ↑                              ↓
                                  [VCU] ←—— [Pedals]       [Regen Braking]
```

---

**Model Answer (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**1. Theoretical full-charge range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_c} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{60 \text{ kWh}}{16 \text{ kWh/100 km}} \times 100 \\
&= 375 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Charging time from 10% to 80% SoC:**

Energy to be delivered:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= (0.80 - 0.10) \times 60 \text{ kWh} \\
&= 42 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Effective charging power:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eff} &= 50 \text{ kW} \times 0.90 = 45 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Charging time:
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ch} &= \dfrac{42 \text{ kWh}}{45 \text{ kW}} \\
&= 0.9333 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 56 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Annual CO₂ savings:**

EV annual consumption:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{EV} &= \dfrac{16 \text{ kWh}}{100 \text{ km}} \times 12{,}000 \text{ km} \\
&= 1{,}920 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

EV annual CO₂:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{EV} &= 1{,}920 \times 0.72 = 1{,}382.4 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Petrol car annual consumption:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{petrol} &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{15} = 800 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Petrol car annual CO₂:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{ICE} &= 800 \times 2.31 = 1{,}848 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual savings:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2 &= 1{,}848 - 1{,}382.4 \\
&= 465.6 \text{ kg CO₂/year} \\
&\approx 0.47 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Final Answers:**
- Range = **375 km**
- Charging time = **~56 minutes**
- Annual CO₂ savings = **~466 kg CO₂/year**

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of E-Mobility in achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**. Critically analyze the **ethical challenges** in battery raw material sourcing and end-of-life recycling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **BEV, PHEV, HEV, and FCEV** across at least **six parameters** in a tabular format. Justify which technology is most suitable for **Kerala's urban and semi-urban transport context** with specific reasoning.

---

**Model Answer (a) — E-Mobility and SDGs:**

E-Mobility directly supports multiple **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**:

- **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy):** EVs are a flexible demand-side resource for renewable integration.
- **SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities):** Reduces urban air pollution (PM2.5, NOₓ), a major health crisis in Indian cities.
- **SDG 13 (Climate Action):** Transport = ~14% of India's GHG emissions. Electrification is critical for the **2070 net-zero** target.
- **SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being):** Eliminates tailpipe emissions, reducing respiratory diseases.
- **SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure):** Drives battery manufacturing innovation (e.g., **Tata's Gigafactory in Gujarat**).

**Ethical Challenges:**

1. **Raw Material Sourcing:** Lithium (Chile, Australia, Bolivia), Cobalt (DRC — 70% global supply, child labor concerns), Nickel (Indonesia, environmental degradation). This raises **ethical questions on supply chain transparency** and the principle of "do no harm across borders."

2. **Battery Manufacturing Footprint:** Cell production is energy-intensive. A **100 kWh battery** has a manufacturing carbon cost of ~**5 – 7 tonnes CO₂**, requiring ~20,000 km of clean-grid driving to amortize.

3. **End-of-Life Recycling:** Currently, only ~**5% of Li-ion batteries** are recycled globally. Improper disposal causes soil and water contamination (heavy metals like cobalt, nickel leach). **Second-life applications** (stationary grid storage) extend useful life by 5–10 years.

4. **Just Transition:** Mining-dependent communities (e.g., Jharkhand, Odisha) need **reskilling and fair compensation** as the ICE supply chain contracts.

5. **Consumer Data Ethics:** Connected EVs collect massive telemetry data. Privacy, ownership of usage data, and cybersecurity need ethical frameworks.

---

**Model Answer (b) — Comparison and Recommendation for Kerala:**

| Parameter | BEV | PHEV | HEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Grid | Grid + Petrol | Petrol | Hydrogen |
| **Tailpipe emissions** | Zero | Zero (EV mode) | Low | Zero (H₂O only) |
| **Range** | 200 – 600 km | 500 – 800 km | 500 – 800 km | 400 – 650 km |
| **Charging/refuel time** | 30 min – 8 h | 3 h + petrol | NA | 5 – 10 min |
| **Operating cost (₹/km)** | 0.8 – 1.5 | 3 – 5 | 5 – 7 | 5 – 8 |
| **Suitability for Kerala** | **High** | Medium | Low | Very Low (no H₂ infra) |
| **Maintenance** | Very low | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Infrastructure need** | Charging stations | Charging + petrol | Petrol only | Hydrogen refueling |
| **Sustainability rating** | High | Medium | Low–Medium | Medium |

**Justification for Kerala:**

**BEV is the most suitable technology for Kerala's urban and semi-urban transport context**, for the following reasons:

1. **Average daily driving in Kerala is ~30 – 50 km** in urban areas (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) and ~70 – 90 km semi-urban — well within BEV range.

2. **Kerala's electricity grid is relatively cleaner** with substantial hydro share (~30% in 2024) and growing rooftop solar adoption.

3. **High humidity and short-trip patterns** favor BEVs over FCEVs (which need expensive H₂ refueling stations).

4. **Kerala's two-wheeler dominance** (over 70% of vehicles) creates a massive opportunity for **electric two-wheeler adoption** (Ola Electric, Ather, Hero Electric) with home charging.

5. **Government support:** Kerala's EV Policy 2021 and KSEB's charging network in Kochi Metro region support BEV growth.

6. **Hydrogen infrastructure is non-existent** in Kerala, making FCEV impractical in the foreseeable future.

7. **Topography consideration:** Kerala's hilly terrain benefits from BEVs' regenerative braking on downhill slopes, partially recharging the battery and increasing real-world range.

✅ **Conclusion:** For Kerala, **BEVs (especially 2W and compact 4W) offer the optimal balance of sustainability, cost, infrastructure readiness, and ethical sourcing**, making them the most defensible choice in a KTU 14-mark answer.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing E-Mobility with EVs alone** — must mention the *system*: grid, charging, recycling, policy.
> 2. **Ignoring "well-to-wheel"** — never claim EVs are "zero emission." Always say "zero tailpipe emission, lifecycle emission depends on grid mix."
> 3. **Skipping the CC-CV charging profile** in numerical questions — real fast charging tapers, but textbooks often ask theoretical values. State the assumption.
> 4. **Mixing up HEV and PHEV** — HEVs cannot be plugged in. PHEVs can. This single confusion costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always write units explicitly: kWh, km, kg CO₂, minutes. Examiners deduct for missing units.
> 6. **In ethics questions, ignoring social/equity dimensions** — E-Mobility sustainability is not just environmental; it includes **accessibility, affordability, and just transition for workers**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **E-Mobility** = vehicles + infrastructure + energy + policy + recycling. Not just cars.
- [x] **4 EV types:** BEV (pure), PHEV (plug-in hybrid), HEV (self-charging hybrid), FCEV (hydrogen).
- [x] **Key components:** Battery Pack, BMS, Inverter, Traction Motor, OBC, VCU, Thermal Management, Regen Braking.
- [x] **Battery energy formula:** $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ (result in Wh, convert to kWh).
- [x] **Range formula:** $R = (E_{bat} / E_c) \times 100$.
- [x] **Charging time:** $t_{ch} = \Delta E / (P_{charger} \times \eta_{ch})$.
- [x] **C-rate:** $C = I / C_{rated}$ (1C = 1 hour full charge).
- [x] **CO₂ savings formula:** $\Delta CO_2 = (V_{petrol} \times EF_{petrol}) - (E_{EV} \times EF_{grid})$.
- [x] **CCS2 is India's DC fast-charging standard.**
- [x] **EV efficiency: 85–95% (motor) vs. ICE: 20–35% (engine).**
- [x] **Well-to-wheel** matters more than tailpipe — coal-powered EV is still cleaner than petrol.
- [x] **Battery recycling is the unsolved ethical frontier** — only ~5% recycled globally.
- [x] **SDG links:** SDG 7, 11, 13 are the core E-Mobility SDGs.
- [x] **India's grid emission factor (CEA 2023):** ~0.72 kg CO₂/kWh.
- [x] **Kerala's grid is ~30% hydro + solar** → BEVs make strong sustainability sense.
- [x] **FAME-II** is India's main EV subsidy scheme; **PM e-Drive** is its 2024 successor.
- [x] **State of Health (SoH)** is the key battery aging metric — EV batteries are typically retired at **70–80% SoH**.
- [x] **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** turns parked EVs into distributed energy storage.
- [x] **Critical minerals (Li, Co, Ni)** are ethical sourcing flashpoints — KTU ethics questions love this.
- [x] **Greenwashing alert:** "100% green EV" claims without specifying the grid source are misleading.
- [x] **2W and 3W (autorickshaws)** are the highest-impact Indian EV segments, not luxury 4W cars.

---

> **End of Module 3 — E-Mobility Notes | UCHUT347 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
