# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modeled on:** KTU University Exam, KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / June 2024 pattern, Module 4.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — **3 Marks**
**Define the term "Wind Energy". Explain the term "Betz Limit" and state its value.**

**Model Answer:**

**Wind Energy** is the kinetic energy possessed by moving air masses in the Earth's atmosphere, which can be **harnessed by wind turbines** and converted into useful mechanical work or, more commonly, electrical power. It is classified as a **renewable** and **non-conventional** energy source because the wind is continuously regenerated by solar radiation.

The **Betz Limit** is the **theoretical maximum fraction of the kinetic energy in the wind that can be extracted by an ideal wind turbine rotor**. It is derived from the actuator-disc model using conservation of mass and momentum. The maximum value of the power coefficient is:

$$C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 59.26\,\%$$

This means **no turbine — however well designed — can ever convert more than 59.26 % of the wind's kinetic energy into mechanical energy.** The remaining energy must pass through the rotor to keep the air flowing.

*(Word count ≈ 110; within the KTU 3-mark answer length of ~100–120 words.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — **3 Marks**
**List any six factors affecting the power output of a wind energy conversion system.**

**Model Answer:**

The power output $P = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ of a WECS depends on:

1. **Wind speed (v)** — Cubic dependence; the most dominant factor.
2. **Air density ($\rho$)** — Higher at lower temperatures and lower altitudes.
3. **Swept area of rotor (A = $\pi R^2$)** — Larger blade radius gives more power.
4. **Power coefficient ($C_P$)** — Depends on blade design, tip-speed ratio, and pitch angle.
5. **Site elevation and topography** — Hills, valleys, and roughness of terrain affect wind profile.
6. **Machine efficiency** — Gearbox, generator, and power-electronics losses.
7. **Capacity factor and availability** — Downtime reduces annual energy.
8. **Wind direction variability and turbulence intensity** — Affects control system and fatigue life.

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (Choose ANY ONE)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** write the formula $P = \rho A v$ or $P = \rho A v^2$. Always show $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ explicitly.
> - **Do not** omit the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor — it costs 1 full mark.
> - **Do not** confuse Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) with Power Coefficient ($C_P$); they are **different** dimensionless numbers.
> - **Always** mention the **cubic dependence on wind speed** when asked about power — examiners specifically look for this phrase.
> - **Always** state the Betz Limit value (16/27) along with the derivation, not just the derivation.
> - For 7-mark sub-parts, **always include a labelled diagram or table**; it secures at least 1 additional mark.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 pattern]

#### (a) Derive the expression for the power available in the wind. How does it vary with wind speed? Discuss the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The derivation follows the standard 5-step procedure given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Below is a concise exam-style write-up.

**Step 1:** Consider a stream of air flowing with uniform velocity $v$ (m/s) through a cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) perpendicular to the flow direction — equal to the rotor's swept area.

**Step 2:** In one second, the volume of air crossing the rotor is $V = A v$ (m³). Using density $\rho$, the **mass flow rate** is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Step 3:** Each kilogram of air carries kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} v^2$. Therefore, the rate of kinetic energy delivery — i.e., the power — is:

$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3\,}$$

**Variation with wind speed:**
The power is **proportional to the cube of wind speed**. Hence:
- Doubling wind speed → **8× power**
- Tripling wind speed → **27× power**

**Practical implications:**

| Implication | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Site selection | A site with 1 m/s higher mean wind speed gives ~30 % more power — dominating design choice. |
| Tower height | Wind speed increases with height (power law), so taller towers capture more energy. |
| Cut-in / cut-out | Turbines start only above ~3–4 m/s and shut down above ~25 m/s for safety. |
| Energy storage | Because wind is intermittent, batteries/hybrid systems are essential. |
| Capacity factor | Real wind farms achieve only 20–40 % CF due to variable wind. |

**Numerical check (optional, for full marks):**
For $v = 10$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A = 5000$ m²:
$$P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(5000)(10)^3 = 3.0625 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 3.06 \text{ MW}$$

#### (b) Explain the concept of Betz Limit. Derive its value. Why is it not achieved in practice? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** The Betz Limit is the **theoretical maximum** fraction of the wind's kinetic power that an **ideal** turbine can extract. It is named after German physicist **Albert Betz** (1919). Its value is $C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$.

**Derivation (summary — see Section 3.2 for full version):**

Using the 1-D actuator disc model with upstream velocity $v_1$, downstream velocity $v_2$, and induction factor $a$ where $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$:

$$C_P(a) = 4a(1 - a)^2$$

Maximising: $\dfrac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0$

The non-trivial optimum is $a = 1/3$. Substituting:

$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

**Why the Betz Limit is not achieved in practice:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Rotor is not an ideal disc | Real blades have finite number, finite tip losses, hub losses. |
| Aerodynamic profile losses | Blade sections cannot be perfectly optimised for all wind speeds. |
| Mechanical losses | Gearbox friction, generator losses, bearing drag. |
| Wake rotation | Real rotors induce swirl in the wake, wasting energy. |
| Wind variability | Gusts and turbulence cause continuous off-design operation. |
| Control limitations | Pitch and yaw systems have finite response times. |

**Conclusion:** Modern utility-scale HAWTs achieve $C_P \approx 0.40$–$0.50$, well below the Betz limit but the highest of any practical wind-energy device.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - June 2023 pattern]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Horizontal-Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) power plant. List its main components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (text-form for handwritten exam):**

```
   [WIND] → [ROTOR + BLADES] → [GEARBOX] → [GENERATOR] → [POWER ELECTRONICS] → [TRANSFORMER] → [GRID]
                ↑                    ↑                ↑                ↑                ↑
            [YAW + PITCH]      [BRAKE SYSTEM]   [SCADA MONITOR]   [PROTECTION]    [SWITCHGEAR]
```

**Main Components and Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Rotor blades (3 typically)** | Capture wind kinetic energy; aerofoil-shaped for lift. |
| **Hub** | Attaches blades to main shaft. |
| **Nacelle** | Houses gearbox, generator, control electronics. |
| **Main shaft (low-speed)** | Transmits rotation from hub to gearbox. |
| **Gearbox** | Steps up rotor rpm (~15–20 rpm) to generator rpm (~1500 rpm). |
| **Generator** | Converts mechanical energy to electrical (usually DFIG or PMSG). |
| **Yaw drive & motor** | Rotates nacelle to face the wind. |
| **Pitch control** | Adjusts blade pitch to regulate power in high winds. |
| **Anemometer & wind vane** | Sense wind speed and direction; feed SCADA. |
| **Tower** | Supports nacelle at hub height (60–120 m typical). |
| **Transformer & switchgear** | Steps up voltage for grid transmission. |
| **SCADA system** | Remote monitoring, fault diagnostics, performance logging. |

**Working Principle:**
1. Wind strikes the aerofoil blades → pressure differential → **lift force** rotates the rotor.
2. The low-speed shaft drives the **gearbox** which multiplies rpm.
3. The high-speed shaft drives the **generator** to produce AC electricity.
4. **Power electronics** condition the output (variable frequency → grid frequency).
5. The **transformer** steps up voltage for transmission to the grid.
6. The **yaw and pitch systems** continuously orient the rotor optimally and protect it from overspeed.

#### (b) Discuss the environmental and ethical advantages of wind energy. What are the major challenges in large-scale deployment in India? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Environmental Advantages:**

| Advantage | Detail |
|---|---|
| Zero operational CO₂ emissions | ~0.5–1.0 kg CO₂-eq avoided per kWh generated. |
| No air pollutants | No SO₂, NOₓ, PM — unlike coal-based thermal plants. |
| Low water consumption | Unlike thermal plants that need massive cooling water. |
| Small land footprint | Only ~1–2 % of wind-farm area is occupied; rest can be farmed. |
| Reversible land use | Decommissioning restores original land condition. |

**Ethical Advantages (Sustainable Development perspective):**

1. **Inter-generational equity** — Wind does not deplete the resource base.
2. **Energy access** — Decentralised wind systems empower rural & remote communities.
3. **Climate-justice** — Reduces India's per-capita emissions, helping meet NDC commitments under the Paris Agreement.
4. **Alignment with SDGs** — Contributes to SDG 7 (Clean Energy), SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities).
5. **Local employment** — Manufacturing, O&M, and transport create green jobs.

**Major Challenges in India:**

| Challenge | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Intermittency** | Wind is not 24×7; needs storage or grid balancing. |
| **Grid integration** | Variable output stresses the existing transmission network. |
| **Land acquisition** | Wind-rich states have dense population; community conflicts. |
| **Single-buyer risk** | Most offtake is via state DISCOMs with weak finances. |
| **Bird & bat mortality** | Rotor blades pose a hazard to avian fauna. |
| **Noise & visual impact** | Local opposition in scenic / residential zones. |
| **Low Capacity Factor** | Indian onshore CF ≈ 20–25 % vs. global best ≈ 45 %. |
| **Manufacturing dependence** | Many critical components still imported. |
| **Storage cost** | Battery storage remains expensive for large-scale use. |

**Conclusion (mandatory for full marks):**
Despite challenges, wind energy remains a **cornerstone of India's clean-energy transition**. With policy support, hybrid storage, and offshore expansion, the sector can scale from ~44 GW to **140 GW by 2030** (MNRE target), directly serving the ethical mandate of *sustainable development*.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision card — print, pin, and revise!**

- ✅ **Wind = kinetic energy of moving air**, caused by uneven solar heating + Coriolis effect + topography.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — **never write without the $\tfrac{1}{2}$**.
- ✅ **Cubic dependence** on wind speed — the single most important phrase in wind-energy answers.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ — derived using the actuator-disc model.
- ✅ **Power extracted:** $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$, with $C_P$ typically 0.40 – 0.50 in modern HAWTs.
- ✅ **Tip-Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — dimensionless; key design parameter.
- ✅ **Swept area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — bigger blades = more power (squared dependence).
- ✅ **Air density (standard):** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at 15 °C, sea level.
- ✅ **HAWT** is dominant for utility-scale; **VAWT** for distributed/urban.
- ✅ **Site selection criteria:** wind speed > 5 m/s, open terrain, grid proximity, environmental clearance.
- ✅ **Cut-in ≈ 3–4 m/s, Cut-out ≈ 25 m/s, Rated ≈ 12–15 m/s** (typical values — memorise).
- ✅ **Capacity Factor of Indian wind farms ≈ 20–25 %** (current).
- ✅ **India: 4th globally in installed wind capacity (~44 GW as of 2023); leader: Tamil Nadu.**
- ✅ **Zero operational emissions; embodied carbon in manufacturing.**
- ✅ **Ethical link:** Inter-generational equity, SDG 7 (Clean Energy), SDG 13 (Climate Action).
- ✅ **Key challenges:** Intermittency, grid integration, land acquisition, bird-bat mortality, low CF.
- ✅ **Memorise 5-step derivation** of $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — it is asked almost every KTU cycle.
- ✅ **Memorise Betz derivation** — substitute $a = 1/3$ in $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ to get 16/27.
- ✅ **Always include a labelled diagram/table** in 7-mark sub-parts for at least 1–2 extra marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner Reminder:**
> Wind-energy questions test **three things** — (i) the cubic power equation, (ii) the Betz limit, and (iii) the **sustainability-ethics linkage**. A candidate who writes a perfect derivation but ignores the environmental/ethical angle typically loses **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always close with a one-line sustainability/ethical conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydro
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHUT347 - ENGINEERING ETHICS AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydro

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 HYDROGEN ENERGY — THE FOUNDATION OF A SUSTAINABLE FUTURE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Energy** refers to the use of **molecular hydrogen (H₂)** as a clean, versatile, and high-energy-density fuel for power generation, transportation, and industrial processes, where the only by-product (when combusted cleanly or used in a fuel cell) is **water (H₂O)** and heat.

Hydrogen is the **lightest** and **most abundant** chemical element in the universe. However, it does not exist freely in nature — it must be extracted from compounds such as water, natural gas, or biomass. When extracted using renewable energy sources, hydrogen becomes a **carbon-neutral** energy carrier — making it central to the global transition toward sustainability.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery of Gases"

Imagine a **glass of clean water**. That water is essentially stored hydrogen — locked inside a chemical bond with oxygen. To unlock it, you pass an electric current through water (a process called **electrolysis**), and the water splits into its two components: hydrogen gas and oxygen gas.

> Think of hydrogen as a **"currency" of energy**:
> - **Electricity** is hard to store for long durations (batteries degrade).
> - **Hydrogen** acts like a portable bank — you can store energy as H₂ when the sun shines or wind blows, then spend it later via fuel cells or turbines when needed.

Hydrogen solves the **"intermittency problem"** of renewables like solar and wind.

### 🎨 Hydrogen Rainbow — Color Coding of Hydrogen

> [!NOTE]
> The colors assigned to hydrogen denote **how it is produced** and the corresponding **carbon footprint**:

| Color | Production Route | Carbon Emissions | Sustainability Grade |
|---|---|---|---|
| ⚫ **Grey Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | High (≈ 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Not Sustainable |
| 🔵 **Blue Hydrogen** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Low–Medium (≈ 1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Transitional |
| 🟢 **Green Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by Renewables | Near-zero (≈ 0 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | **Fully Sustainable** |
| 🟡 **Yellow Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by Grid electricity | Variable | Mixed |
| 🟤 **Brown Hydrogen** | Coal Gasification | Very High | Highly Polluting |
| 🟣 **Pink Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by Nuclear | Near-zero | Sustainable |

### 🔑 Why Hydrogen Energy Matters in Engineering Ethics & Sustainability

1. **Decarbonization** — replaces fossil fuels in "hard-to-abate" sectors (steel, cement, shipping, aviation).
2. **Energy Security** — diversified fuel sources reduce geopolitical dependence on oil.
3. **Universal Energy Carrier** — can be used as gas, liquid (LH₂), or carrier (ammonia, methanol).
4. **Ethics of Climate Justice** — developing nations gain access to clean energy without sacrificing development.
5. **Engineer's Responsibility** — designing safe hydrogen systems prevents accidents (hydrogen is highly flammable and odorless).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 📐 Fundamental Properties of Hydrogen

Hydrogen has three isotopes: **Protium (¹H)**, **Deuterium (²H or D)**, and **Tritium (³H or T)**. For energy applications, **Protium** is used.

### 🔬 Key Physical & Thermochemical Properties

- **Atomic Number:** 1
- **Molecular Weight:** 2.016 g/mol
- **Density (at STP):** 0.0899 kg/m³ (≈ 14× lighter than air)
- **Boiling Point:** −252.87 °C (must be liquefied for storage)
- **Energy Density (by mass):** **142 MJ/kg** — ≈ 3× higher than gasoline (≈ 46 MJ/kg)
- **Energy Density (by volume):** Only **8.5 MJ/L** (liquid) — much lower than fossil fuels → storage challenge
- **Flammability Range in Air:** 4% to 75% by volume (very wide → high risk of explosion)
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** 500 °C
- **Flame Speed:** 8–10× faster than gasoline
- **Calorific Value (HHV):** 142 MJ/kg
- **Calorific Value (LHV):** 120 MJ/kg

## ⚙️ Hydrogen Production Pathways — Detailed Theory

### A. Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Grey Hydrogen

**Step 1 — Reforming Reaction:**
Methane reacts with steam at high temperature (700–1000 °C) under a nickel catalyst.

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
Carbon monoxide reacts with more steam to produce additional hydrogen.

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Overall Reaction:** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$
>
> For every 1 kg of hydrogen produced via SMR, **9–10 kg of CO₂** is released — making it environmentally unsustainable.

### B. Electrolysis of Water — Green Hydrogen

When an electric current is passed through water (with an electrolyte), it splits into hydrogen and oxygen.

**At Cathode (Reduction):**
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$$

**At Anode (Oxidation):**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +286 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Electrolyzers:**
> - **Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL):** Mature, low cost, 60–70% efficiency
> - **PEM Electrolyzer:** Fast response, high purity, 70–80% efficiency
> - **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEL):** Highest efficiency (~85%) but high temperature operation

### C. Biomass Gasification — Bio-Hydrogen

Organic waste is gasified at high temperatures to produce a syngas rich in hydrogen.

$$\text{Biomass} + \text{Heat} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Tars}$$

### D. Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting — Artificial Leaf

Sunlight directly splits water using semiconductor photocatalysts. Still in research stage.

## 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydrogen Energy Calculations

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Energy from Combustion | $E = m \times \text{HHV}$ | $m$ = mass of H₂ burned (kg); $E$ in MJ |
| Hydrogen from Electrolysis (Faraday's Law) | $m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency; $I$ = current (A); $t$ = time (s); $M = 2.016$ g/mol; $n = 2$ electrons; $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| Theoretical Voltage for Water Splitting | $E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}$ | Minimum voltage required at 25 °C |
| Thermoneutral Voltage | $V_{tn} = 1.481 \text{ V}$ | Voltage where no net heat exchange occurs |
| Round-Trip Efficiency (PtG-PtP) | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{elec} \times \eta_{FC}$ | $\eta_{elec}$ = electrolyzer efficiency; $\eta_{FC}$ = fuel cell efficiency |
| Hydrogen Liquefaction Energy | $E_{liq} \approx 0.40 \times \text{LHV}$ | ~40% of LHV is needed to liquefy H₂ |
| Calorific Value Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{HHV}_{\text{H}_2}}{\text{HHV}_{\text{gasoline}}} = \dfrac{142}{46} \approx 3.09$ | Hydrogen carries 3× more energy per kg |
| Specific Energy | $\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Measured in MJ/kg |
| Energy Density (Volume) | $\rho_E = \rho_m \times \text{LHV}$ | $\rho_m$ = mass density; gives volumetric energy density |
| Carbon Intensity (SMR) | $\text{CI}_{\text{SMR}} = 9.5 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg H}_2$ | Standard emission factor for grey hydrogen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Whenever hydrogen is mentioned in the context of sustainability, the answer must always highlight **Green Hydrogen** as the ethical and sustainable choice. Examiners look for the candidate's ability to compare production methods on the basis of **carbon footprint**, **energy efficiency**, and **life-cycle emissions**.

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application | Current Status |
|---|---|---|
| 🚗 **Transportation** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo | Commercial |
| 🏭 **Industry** | Green steel production (replacing coal in blast furnaces) | Pilot scale |
| ⚡ **Power Generation** | Hydrogen gas turbines, stationary fuel cells | Demonstrated |
| 🚢 **Shipping** | Ammonia & hydrogen-fueled cargo ships | Pilot stage |
| ✈️ **Aviation** | Hydrogen-powered aircraft (e.g., ZEROe by Airbus) | R&D |
| 🏠 **Heating** | Hydrogen blending in natural gas networks (up to 20%) | Demonstrated |
| 💾 **Energy Storage** | Seasonal storage of surplus renewable energy | Growing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 📝 Derivation 1 — Mass of Hydrogen Produced by Electrolysis

**Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** states that the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q$$

$$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

Where $Z$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** (mass deposited per unit charge):

$$Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

Substituting:

$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Adding efficiency factor $\eta$:

$$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}}$$

### 🧪 Worked Example 1

> **Problem:** A PEM electrolyzer operates at a current of **500 A** for **10 hours** with an efficiency of **75%**. Calculate the mass of hydrogen produced. Take $M = 2.016$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Given:
- $I = 500$ A
- $t = 10 \text{ hr} = 10 \times 3600 = 36000$ s
- $\eta = 0.75$
- $M = 2.016$ g/mol
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96485$ C/mol

**Step 1:** Calculate total charge passed:

$$Q = I \times t = 500 \times 36000 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate theoretical mass without efficiency:

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{2.016 \times 1.8 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{3.6288 \times 10^7}{192970} = 188.04 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3:** Apply efficiency factor:

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times m_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.75 \times 188.04 = 141.03 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2} \approx 141.03 \text{ g} \approx 0.141 \text{ kg}}$$

---

## 📝 Derivation 2 — Energy Output from Hydrogen Combustion

The energy released during complete combustion of hydrogen follows the relation:

$$\text{H}_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$

The energy released per kg is given by:

$$E = m \times \text{HHV}$$

### 🧪 Worked Example 2

> **Problem:** A hydrogen-powered bus carries **40 kg** of liquid hydrogen. If the fuel cell operates at **55% efficiency**, calculate the useful electrical energy produced (use HHV = 142 MJ/kg).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total chemical energy stored:

$$E_{\text{chemical}} = m \times \text{HHV} = 40 \times 142 = 5680 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2:** Useful electrical energy after efficiency losses:

$$E_{\text{useful}} = \eta \times E_{\text{chemical}} = 0.55 \times 5680$$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 3124 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$E_{\text{useful}} = \dfrac{3124}{3.6} \approx 867.8 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{useful}} \approx 3124 \text{ MJ} \approx 867.8 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

## 📝 Derivation 3 — Round-Trip Efficiency of Power-to-Gas-to-Power (PtG-PtP)

When hydrogen is used for energy storage, the **round-trip efficiency** determines how much of the original electricity is recovered.

**Stage 1 — Electrolysis Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{\text{HHV of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Electrical energy input}}$$

**Stage 2 — Fuel Cell Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{\text{Electrical energy output}}{\text{HHV of H}_2 \text{ consumed}}$$

**Round-Trip Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_{elec} \times \eta_{FC}$$

### 🧪 Worked Example 3

> **Problem:** A grid storage system uses an electrolyzer with **70% efficiency** to produce hydrogen, which is later fed to a fuel cell with **60% efficiency**. If **100 kWh** of surplus electricity is stored, calculate the electricity recovered.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate round-trip efficiency:

$$\eta_{RT} = 0.70 \times 0.60 = 0.42 = 42\%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate electricity recovered:

$$E_{\text{recovered}} = \eta_{RT} \times E_{\text{input}} = 0.42 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{recovered}} = 42 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

## 📝 Derivation 4 — Comparison of Energy Released: H₂ vs Gasoline

> **Problem:** Compare the energy released by **1 kg of H₂** vs **1 kg of gasoline**.

**Step 1 — Hydrogen:**
$$E_{\text{H}_2} = 1 \times 142 = 142 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Gasoline:**
$$E_{\text{gasoline}} = 1 \times 46 = 46 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\dfrac{E_{\text{H}_2}}{E_{\text{gasoline}}} = \dfrac{142}{46} = 3.087$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hydrogen releases } 3.09\times \text{ more energy per kg than gasoline}}$$

**However**, due to its low density, **1 L of liquid H₂** contains only **8.5 MJ** vs gasoline's **≈ 34 MJ/L**. This is why hydrogen storage volume is a major engineering challenge.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Hydrogen vs Other Energy Carriers

| Parameter | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Gasoline | Lithium Battery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy by Mass (MJ/kg) | 142 | 55 | 46 | 0.5–1.0 |
| Energy by Volume (MJ/L) | 8.5 (liq) | 22 (gas) | 34 | 1.5–2.5 |
| Carbon Emissions | 0 (green) | High | High | Indirect |
| Storage Duration | Long (months) | Long | Long | Limited |
| Refueling Time | 5–10 min | N/A | 2 min | 30 min–hours |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 35–45% | 30–40% | 20–30% | 85–95% |
| Safety Concerns | High (leaks, explosion) | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔄 Diagram 1 — Hydrogen Production & Utilization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[Renewable Energy Sources] -->|Solar Power| elecSys
    sourceB[Wind Power] -->|Wind Energy| elecSys
    sourceC[Hydropower] -->|Hydro Energy| elecSys
    sourceD[Biomass] -->|Bio Route| bioSys
    sourceE[Natural Gas] -->|Fossil Route| smrSys
    elecSys[Electrolyzer System] -->|Green Hydrogen| storageHub
    bioSys[Biomass Gasifier] -->|Bio Hydrogen| storageHub
    smrSys[Steam Methane Reformer] -->|Grey Hydrogen| storageHub
    storageHub[Hydrogen Storage and Transport Hub] --> pipe1[Compressed Gas Pipeline]
    storageHub --> pipe2[Cryogenic Liquid Tank]
    storageHub --> pipe3[Ammonia Carrier Route]
    pipe1 --> endUser1[Industry Sector]
    pipe2 --> endUser2[Transport FCEV]
    pipe3 --> endUser3[Power Generation]
    endUser1 --> output1[Water Vapor Only]
    endUser2 --> output2[Water Vapor Only]
    endUser3 --> output3[Water Vapor Only]
    output1 --> cycleBack[Return to Environment]
    output2 --> cycleBack
    output3 --> cycleBack
    cycleBack --> sourceA
```

> **Caption:** The schematic illustrates the closed-loop hydrogen economy. Note that **only water vapor** is released when green hydrogen is utilized — completing a true zero-carbon cycle.

---

## ⚡ Diagram 2 — PEM Fuel Cell Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet1[Hydrogen Inlet] --> anodeSide[Anode Side Catalyst Layer]
    inlet2[Oxygen or Air Inlet] --> cathodeSide[Cathode Side Catalyst Layer]
    anodeSide --> reaction1[H2 splits into 2H plus and 2 electrons]
    reaction1 --> electronPath[Electrons forced through external circuit producing electricity]
    reaction1 --> protonPath[Protons migrate through PEM membrane to cathode]
    electronPath --> externalLoad[External Electrical Load]
    externalLoad --> electronReturn[Electrons return to cathode]
    protonPath --> cathodeSide
    electronReturn --> cathodeSide
    cathodeSide --> reaction2[O2 plus 4H plus plus 4e minus forms 2H2O]
    reaction2 --> outlet1[Water and Heat Outlet]
```

> **Caption:** Block diagram of a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) Fuel Cell**. The PEM allows only protons (H⁺) to pass — electrons are forced through the external circuit, generating usable electric current.

---

## 🏭 Diagram 3 — Hydrogen Economy Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph productionBlock[Production Stage]
        p1[Green Electrolysis]
        p2[Blue SMR plus CCS]
        p3[Grey SMR Conventional]
    end
    subgraph conditioningBlock[Conditioning Stage]
        c1[Compression to 700 bar]
        c2[Liquefaction to minus 253 C]
        c3[Conversion to Ammonia]
    end
    subgraph storageBlock[Storage and Transport Stage]
        s1[Underground Salt Caverns]
        s2[Cryogenic Tankers]
        s3[Pipeline Networks]
    end
    subgraph utilizationBlock[End Use Applications]
        u1[FCEV Transportation]
        u2[Industrial Feedstock]
        u3[Power Generation]
        u4[Residential Heating]
    end
    productionBlock --> conditioningBlock
    conditioningBlock --> storageBlock
    storageBlock --> utilizationBlock
```

> **Caption:** The complete **Hydrogen Value Chain** — from production through conditioning, storage, transport, to end-use application. This is a standard conceptual framework expected in KTU board examinations.

---

## 🌱 Diagram 4 — Sustainability Comparison: Hydrogen Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startChoice{Hydrogen Production Choice} --> greenRoute[Green Hydrogen Route]
    startChoice --> blueRoute[Blue Hydrogen Route]
    startChoice --> greyRoute[Grey Hydrogen Route]
    greenRoute --> greenImpact[Carbon Emissions Near Zero]
    greenRoute --> greenScore[Sustainability Score 9 of 10]
    blueRoute --> blueImpact[Carbon Emissions 1 to 3 kg CO2 per kg H2]
    blueRoute --> blueScore[Sustainability Score 6 of 10]
    greyRoute --> greyImpact[Carbon Emissions 9 to 10 kg CO2 per kg H2]
    greyRoute --> greyScore[Sustainability Score 2 of 10]
    greenImpact --> verdict[Best Choice for Long Term Sustainability]
    blueImpact --> verdict
    greyImpact --> verdict
```

> **Caption:** A sustainability scoring visualization contrasting the three dominant hydrogen pathways. **Green hydrogen** is the only ethically defensible choice for long-term sustainable development.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define Green Hydrogen. Explain why it is considered the most sustainable form of hydrogen energy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Green hydrogen is hydrogen produced by the **electrolysis of water using electricity generated from renewable sources** such as solar, wind, hydropower, or geothermal energy. No fossil fuels are used in the production process.

**Production Process (1 Mark):**
The overall reaction is $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$, powered entirely by renewable electricity. The electrolyzer (PEM, Alkaline, or SOEL) splits water into hydrogen and oxygen gas without any carbon emissions.

**Why It Is Sustainable (1 Mark):**
- Carbon footprint is **near-zero** (≈ 0 kg CO₂ per kg H₂)
- Uses **abundant and renewable** inputs (water + sunlight/wind)
- Enables **seasonal storage** of renewable energy
- Forms a **closed loop** — combustion/usage produces only water, which can be re-electrolyzed

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **List and briefly explain any three challenges in the widespread adoption of hydrogen as a fuel.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Storage Challenge:** Hydrogen has very low volumetric energy density. It must be either compressed to **700 bar**, liquefied at **−253 °C**, or converted to carriers like **ammonia**. All these methods require significant energy and expensive infrastructure.

2. **Production Cost:** Green hydrogen currently costs **USD 3–6 per kg**, whereas grey hydrogen costs only **USD 1–2 per kg**. Until electrolyzer costs drop and renewable electricity becomes cheaper, green hydrogen cannot compete economically.

3. **Safety Concerns:** Hydrogen is **odorless, colorless, highly flammable** (4–75% flammability range in air), and has a flame speed **8–10× faster than gasoline**. Leak detection is difficult, and embrittlement of metals poses engineering risks.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

> **(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the working of a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Discuss the role of hydrogen in achieving **Sustainable Development Goal 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)**. Compare green, blue, and grey hydrogen on the basis of carbon footprint. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Working Principle of PEM Fuel Cell (4 Marks):**

A PEM fuel cell converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electricity, with water and heat as by-products.

**Step 1 — Hydrogen Supply:**
Hydrogen gas is fed to the **anode** side of the cell. At the anode catalyst (typically platinum), hydrogen molecules split into protons and electrons:

$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

**Step 2 — Proton Migration:**
The **proton exchange membrane** allows only the **protons (H⁺)** to pass through to the cathode side. The electrons are blocked by the membrane and forced to travel through the **external circuit**, producing electrical current.

**Step 3 — Oxygen Reaction at Cathode:**
At the cathode, oxygen (from air) combines with the arriving protons and electrons to form water:

$$\dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4 — By-products:**
Water and heat are expelled from the cathode. The overall reaction is:

$$\text{H}_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$$

**Block Diagram (Reference Diagram 2 in Section 4)**

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
- **High efficiency** (50–60%) compared to internal combustion engines (25–35%)
- **Zero emissions** — only water and heat released
- **Low operating temperature** (60–100 °C) → quick start-up
- **Modular design** → scalable from mobile phones to power plants
- **Silent operation** — no moving parts in the cell

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- **Expensive platinum catalyst**
- **Membrane dehydration issues** at high temperatures
- **Sensitivity to CO** contamination in hydrogen feed
- **Hydrogen storage and refueling infrastructure** is still under development

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Role of Hydrogen in SDG 7 (3 Marks):**

Sustainable Development Goal 7 aims to ensure **affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all**. Hydrogen plays a pivotal role by:

- **Decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors** (steel, shipping, aviation) where direct electrification is not feasible
- **Enabling long-duration energy storage** — surplus solar/wind energy can be stored as hydrogen for months
- **Empowering rural communities** through decentralized hydrogen microgrids
- **Supporting clean cooking fuels** in developing regions (e.g., hydrogen-enriched biogas)
- **Driving green industrial growth** — green ammonia, green methanol, green steel

**Comparison of Hydrogen Pathways on Carbon Footprint (4 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

| Parameter | Green H₂ | Blue H₂ | Grey H₂ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Method** | Electrolysis (renewable) | SMR + CCS | SMR (no CCS) |
| **CO₂ Emissions (kg CO₂/kg H₂)** | ≈ 0 | 1–3 | 9–10 |
| **Sustainability** | Fully sustainable | Transitional | Not sustainable |
| **Cost (USD/kg)** | 3–6 | 2–4 | 1–2 |

**Conclusion:**
Only **green hydrogen** aligns with SDG 7's vision of clean energy for all. Blue hydrogen serves as a transitional bridge, while grey hydrogen must be phased out in line with the **Paris Agreement's 1.5 °C target**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process with chemical equations. Why is it classified as **grey hydrogen** and not sustainable in the long run? **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A PEM electrolyzer operates at **1000 A** current for **24 hours** with an efficiency of **80%**. Calculate the mass of green hydrogen produced. If this hydrogen is used in a fuel cell with **55% efficiency**, determine the useful electrical energy generated. **(Take HHV = 142 MJ/kg, M = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol)** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Steam Methane Reforming Process (4 Marks):**

SMR is the most widely used industrial method for hydrogen production, accounting for **≈ 70–80%** of global hydrogen production.

**Step 1 — Reforming (Primary Reaction):**
Methane is reacted with steam at **700–1000 °C** in the presence of a **nickel-based catalyst**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{Ni cat.}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
The carbon monoxide produced is further reacted with steam to produce additional hydrogen:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe cat.}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Overall Reaction:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$$

**Why Classified as Grey Hydrogen (2 Marks):**

- For every **1 kg** of hydrogen produced, **9–10 kg of CO₂** is released as a by-product.
- The process depends entirely on **natural gas (a fossil fuel)** — a non-renewable resource.
- **No carbon capture** is used — hence the term "grey" (no environmental consideration).
- It is **not aligned with SDG 13 (Climate Action)** or SDG 7 (Clean Energy).
- Long-term reliance on SMR is unsustainable because it locks in **carbon-intensive infrastructure** for decades.

**Sustainability Alternative (1 Mark):**
If the CO₂ released in SMR is captured and stored (CCS), the pathway becomes **Blue Hydrogen** — a transitional step toward sustainability.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $I = 1000$ A
- $t = 24 \text{ hr} = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s
- $\eta_{elec} = 0.80$
- $\eta_{FC} = 0.55$
- $M = 2.016$ g/mol
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- HHV = 142 MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Total Charge Passed:**

$$Q = I \times t = 1000 \times 86400 = 8.64 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Mass of H₂ (Faraday's Law):**

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{M \times Q}{n \times F} = \dfrac{2.016 \times 8.64 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{1.7418 \times 10^8}{192970} = 902.6 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Mass of H₂ Produced (with efficiency):**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{elec} \times m_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.80 \times 902.6$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2} = 722.08 \text{ g} \approx 0.722 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Chemical Energy Stored:**

$$E_{\text{chemical}} = m_{\text{actual}} \times \text{HHV} = 0.722 \times 142 = 102.5 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Useful Electrical Energy from Fuel Cell:**

$$E_{\text{useful}} = \eta_{FC} \times E_{\text{chemical}} = 0.55 \times 102.5$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{useful}} \approx 56.4 \text{ MJ} \approx 15.67 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Green and Grey Hydrogen Definitions** — Many students confuse "blue" with "green". Always remember: **Green = Renewable Electrolysis**, **Blue = SMR + CCS**, **Grey = Plain SMR**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Unit Conversions in Electrolysis Problems** — The most common error is using $t$ in hours instead of seconds. Always convert: **hours × 3600 = seconds**. Failing this costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 3. **Not Showing the Efficiency Step** — Students calculate theoretical mass but forget to multiply by $\eta$. Examiners specifically look for this step.
>
> 4. **Confusing HHV and LHV** — KTU problems may specify which value to use. Default to **HHV = 142 MJ/kg** unless stated otherwise.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Ethics Dimension** — In UCHUT347, marks are awarded for **linking engineering decisions to sustainability and ethics**. A purely technical answer without ethical reasoning will lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Water By-Product** — Always mention that hydrogen combustion/fuel cells produce only **water** — this is the most important sustainability point.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### 🔑 Core Concepts

- **Hydrogen** is the lightest element and the universe's most abundant fuel.
- It is an **energy carrier**, not an energy source — energy must be spent to extract it.
- **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**; **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**; 1 kg H₂ = 3× energy of gasoline.
- **Low volumetric density** → storage challenge (compression, liquefaction, carriers).
- **Wide flammability range** (4–75%) → high safety risk.

### 🌈 Hydrogen Color Spectrum

- ⚫ **Grey** — SMR, 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (NOT sustainable)
- 🔵 **Blue** — SMR + CCS, 1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (Transitional)
- 🟢 **Green** — Electrolysis + Renewables, ≈ 0 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (Sustainable ✅)
- 🟤 **Brown** — Coal gasification (Highly polluting)
- 🟣 **Pink** — Nuclear-powered electrolysis

### ⚙️ Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

$$E = m \times \text{HHV}$$

$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_{elec} \times \eta_{FC}$$

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$$

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$

### 🎓 SDG Linkage

- **SDG 7** — Affordable & Clean Energy
- **SDG 13** — Climate Action
- **SDG 9** — Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure

### ⚖️ Ethical Dimensions

- Engineer's duty to choose **lowest-carbon** option available
- **Energy justice** — clean energy access for developing nations
- **Inter-generational equity** — today's choices affect future climate
- **Precautionary principle** — handle hydrogen safely due to flammability

### 🏭 Applications to Remember

| Application | Hydrogen Form Used | Status |
|---|---|---|
| FCEV Cars | Compressed H₂ (700 bar) | Commercial |
| Steel Production | Green H₂ (replacing coal) | Pilot |
| Shipping Fuel | Ammonia (H₂ carrier) | Pilot |
| Power Backup | Liquid H₂ / LH₂ | Demonstrated |
| Aircraft | Liquid H₂ | R&D |

### 🧠 Key Mnemonic for KTU Exam

> **"H-C-O-L-O-R"** for Hydrogen Colors:
> - **H**eat-generating
> - **C**lean (only when green)
> - **O**dorless, colorless
> - **L**ightest element
> - **O**nly water as emission (green)
> - **R**enewable when sourced from renewables
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: BIOMASS — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIOMASS (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Biomass is defined as any organic matter derived from recently living plants, animals, or their metabolic by-products that can be converted into usable forms of energy — primarily heat, electricity, and liquid/gaseous biofuels. In the KTU 2024 Engineering Ethics and Sustainable Development framework, biomass is classified as a **carbon-neutral renewable energy resource** because the CO₂ released during its combustion/utilization is reabsorbed by the next generation of plants during photosynthesis, completing a closed ecological loop.

In strict scientific and engineering terms (per the **KTU UCHUT347 Module 4** syllabus), biomass refers to the biodegradable fraction of products, waste, and residues from:
- **Agriculture** (crop residues, straw, husks, bagasse)
- **Forestry** (wood logs, sawdust, bark, forestry thinnings)
- **Aquaculture & Animal Husbandry** (manure, animal fats, aquatic algae)
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** (organic fraction — kitchen waste, sewage sludge)
- **Industrial Biowaste** (paper pulp waste, food processing waste)

The chemical composition of biomass is essentially a mixture of three polymers:
- **Cellulose** (a linear polymer of β-D-glucose units, ~40–60% of dry weight)
- **Hemicellulose** (a branched heteropolymer of pentoses and hexoses, ~20–40%)
- **Lignin** (a complex aromatic polymer, ~10–30%)

Plus minor amounts of extractives (resins, waxes, fats) and inorganic ash.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Natural Battery" of the Earth

> [!NOTE]
> **The Plant-as-Battery Analogy:**
> Imagine every green plant on Earth is a tiny rechargeable battery that nature has been charging daily using the sun. When we burn biomass (discharge the battery), we release the stored solar energy as heat. Then, new plants grow (recharging the battery) and absorb the CO₂ we just emitted. This continuous **charge–discharge cycle** is what makes biomass a *renewable* and *theoretically carbon-neutral* energy source — unlike fossil fuels, which are "dead batteries" (ancient biomass buried for millions of years) that take millions of years to recharge.

A simpler way to remember it: **Biomass = Stored Sunlight + Air (CO₂) + Water + Soil Nutrients.** When we burn it, we return what the plant borrowed.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **standard engineering parameters** are mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|-----------|--------------|-------|
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of dry wood** | **16–18** | **MJ/kg** |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) of dry wood | 18–21 | MJ/kg |
| Net Calorific Value of bagasse | 7–9 | MJ/kg |
| Net Calorific Value of rice husk | 12–15 | MJ/kg |
| Methane yield from anaerobic digestion | 0.2–0.4 | m³/kg VS |
| Density of wood pellets | 600–750 | kg/m³ |
| Moisture content of fresh biomass | 30–60 | % wet basis |
| Photosynthetic efficiency of biomass | 1–3 | % of solar input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight for KTU 2024:**
> The term "**biomass**" in the KTU syllabus does NOT include fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas) because those are not part of the contemporary biological carbon cycle. The carbon cycle closure time for biomass is **annually to decadal**, while for fossil fuels it is **geological (millions of years)**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Biomass (Syllabus-Mandated)

Biomass is classified along four major axes in the KTU UCHUT347 framework:

**A. By Origin**
1. **Ligno-cellulosic biomass** — wood, agricultural residues, grasses
2. **Sugar/starch crops** — sugarcane, corn, sorghum (food-crop-based)
3. **Oil-seed crops** — Jatropha, soybean, rapeseed (for biodiesel)
4. **Aquatic biomass** — microalgae, macroalgae, water hyacinth
5. **Animal/human waste** — manure, sewage, slaughterhouse waste

**B. By Physical State**
1. **Solid biomass** — wood chips, pellets, briquettes
2. **Liquid biofuels** — bioethanol, biodiesel, bio-oil (pyrolysis oil)
3. **Gaseous biofuels** — biogas, syngas, producer gas

**C. By Generation (Conventional KTU Classification)**
1. **1st Generation** — food crops (corn ethanol, sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel)
2. **2nd Generation** — non-food lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural waste
3. **3rd Generation** — algae-based biofuels
4. **4th Generation** — engineered biomass / synthetic biology

**D. By Moisture Content**
1. **Dry biomass** (<20% moisture) — wood, straw, pellets
2. **Wet biomass** (>60% moisture) — manure, aquatic biomass, food waste

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4: BIOMASS — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Biomass Matters — The Engineering & Ethical Rationale

From a **sustainable development** perspective, biomass is uniquely positioned because it simultaneously addresses:
- **Energy security** (indigenous, decentralized resource)
- **Climate change mitigation** (carbon-neutral combustion)
- **Rural development** (creates agricultural jobs)
- **Waste management** (valorises agricultural and municipal waste)
- **Ethical dimensions** (food vs. fuel debate — 1st vs. 2nd generation)

## 2.2 The Chemistry of Biomass — A Structured Breakdown

The energy stored in biomass originates from **photosynthesis**, summarized by the equation:

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\;light\;} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

This single reaction is the foundation of all biomass energy. Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) is the primary energy carrier, and is then polymerized by the plant into cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Biomass Energy Release

1. **Step 1 — Solar Capture:** Photons strike chlorophyll → electrons excited → ATP/NADPH generated (Calvin cycle).
2. **Step 2 — Carbon Fixation:** CO₂ is reduced to triose phosphate → converted to glucose → polymerized to cellulose/lignin.
3. **Step 3 — Storage:** Energy is locked in C–C and C–H bonds of organic polymers (bond energy ~350 kJ/mol for C–C).
4. **Step 4 — Conversion:** When biomass is burned, digested, or pyrolysed, these bonds are broken and energy is released as heat, electricity, or fuel.
5. **Step 5 — Carbon Recycle:** Released CO₂ is reabsorbed by new biomass in step 1 → closed loop.

## 2.3 Biomass Conversion Technologies (KTU 2024 — High-Yield Topic)

The KTU syllabus mandates knowledge of four primary conversion routes:

### 2.3.1 Direct Combustion (Thermochemical Route)

The oldest and most common method. Biomass is burned in excess air (or oxygen) to produce heat.

$$C_xH_yO_z + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + Heat$$

- **Efficiency:** 60–90% in modern boilers
- **Products:** Heat (steam) → electricity via Rankine cycle
- **Best Feedstock:** Dry biomass (wood chips, pellets, husk)
- **Drawback:** Air emissions (NOₓ, particulates) if poorly controlled

### 2.3.2 Pyrolysis (Thermochemical Route)

Heating biomass in the **absence of oxygen** at 300–800°C to produce:
- **Biochar** (solid, ~30%)
- **Bio-oil** (liquid, ~30–60%)
- **Syngas** (gas, CO + H₂ + CH₄, ~20%)

The yield depends on temperature and heating rate:
- **Slow pyrolysis** → more biochar
- **Fast pyrolysis** → more bio-oil
- **Gasification** → more syngas

### 2.3.3 Gasification (Thermochemical Route)

Partial oxidation of biomass at 700–1200°C to produce **Producer Gas** (syngas):

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
$$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \text{(Boudouard reaction)}$$
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas reaction)}$$

The producer gas has a calorific value of **4–6 MJ/Nm³**, suitable for engines and turbines.

### 2.3.4 Anaerobic Digestion (Biochemical Route)

Microbial breakdown of organic matter in oxygen-free conditions, producing **biogas**:

$$C_xH_yO_z + H_2O \xrightarrow{\;microbes\;} CH_4 + CO_2 + H_2S + NH_3$$

- **Biogas composition:** 50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, trace H₂S
- **Calorific value of biogas:** ~20–25 MJ/m³
- **Digestate:** nutrient-rich slurry usable as fertilizer

### 2.3.5 Fermentation (Biochemical Route)

Sugar/starch crops are fermented by yeast to produce **bioethanol**:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\;yeast\;} 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$$

Bioethanol has a calorific value of **~29.7 MJ/kg** and is blended with petrol (E10, E20, E85).

### 2.3.6 Transesterification (Chemical Route)

Vegetable oils or animal fats are reacted with methanol (in the presence of NaOH/KOH catalyst) to produce **biodiesel** (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Ester):

$$Triglyceride + 3CH_3OH \rightarrow 3RCOOCH_3 + Glycerol$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 2024 Examination will test the following equations directly or in numerical form. Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right)$ | Lower heating value from hydrogen content (Dulong-like) | MJ/kg |
| 2 | $CV_{biomass} = 0.3491 \cdot C + 1.1783 \cdot H + 0.1005 \cdot S - 0.1034 \cdot O - 0.0151 \cdot N - 0.0211 \cdot A$ | Dulong's formula for biomass calorific value | MJ/kg |
| 3 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{m_f \cdot CV_{biomass}}{m_f \cdot CV_{biomass} + Q_{losses}}$ | Combustion efficiency | % (decimal) |
| 4 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{comb} \cdot \eta_{boiler} \cdot \eta_{Rankine}$ | Overall plant efficiency | % |
| 5 | $E_{out} = m \cdot CV \cdot \eta$ | Useful energy from biomass of mass $m$ | MJ |
| 6 | $CO_{2\,saved} = m_f \cdot \left(CC_{fossil} - CC_{biomass}\right)$ | CO₂ emission savings per kg fuel | kg CO₂ |
| 7 | $CC = 3.67 \cdot \%C_{fuel} \cdot \dfrac{1}{CV_{fuel}}$ | Carbon coefficient of fuel | tCO₂/TJ |
| 8 | $Y_{biogas} = Y_0 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{TS_{in} - TS_{out}}{TS_{in}}\right)$ | Biogas yield from anaerobic digester | m³/kg VS |
| 9 | $\rho_{pellet} = \dfrac{m_{pellet}}{V_{pellet}}$ | Density of biomass pellet | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} \times 100\%$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |

> **Symbols:** C, H, O, N, S, A = mass percentages of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Nitrogen, Sulfur, Ash; $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water = **2.442 MJ/kg**; $m_f$ = mass of fuel; $CC$ = Carbon Coefficient; $Y_0$ = theoretical methane yield; $TS$ = total solids.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Biomass in Engineering

| Sector | Application | Engineering Role |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| **Power generation** | Bagasse cogeneration in sugar mills (India, Brazil) | Mechanical + Electrical engineers |
| **Transportation** | E10/E20 ethanol blending, B100 biodiesel | Chemical + Automotive engineers |
| **Heating** | Wood pellet boilers in cold-climate countries | Civil + Mechanical engineers |
| **Rural electrification** | Off-grid biomass gasifier + 100 kW IC engine set | Electrical engineers |
| **Waste management** | Anaerobic digestion of municipal wet waste | Environmental + Civil engineers |
| **Industrial process heat** | Rice husk furnace, black liquor recovery | Chemical + Mechanical engineers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4: BIOMASS — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Calorific Value of Biomass from Ultimate Analysis (Dulong's Formula)

The Dulong formula is the cornerstone equation of biomass energetics. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know its derivation and application.

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

The heating value of a fuel is the sum of the heats of combustion of its individual combustible elements (C, H, S), minus the heat lost in vaporizing the moisture produced by H-combustion and the moisture originally present in the fuel.

The heats of combustion of pure elements (per kg) are:
- Carbon (C → CO₂): 33,820 kJ/kg
- Hydrogen (H → H₂O liquid): 144,200 kJ/kg
- Sulfur (S → SO₂): 9,260 kJ/kg

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider 1 kg of biomass containing:
- Mass fraction of C = $C/100$ (kg C)
- Mass fraction of H = $H/100$ (kg H)
- Mass fraction of S = $S/100$ (kg S)
- Mass fraction of O = $O/100$ (kg O)
- Mass fraction of N = $N/100$ (kg N)
- Mass fraction of ash = $A/100$ (kg ash)

**Step 2:** Heat released by carbon combustion:
$$Q_C = 33820 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} = 338.2 \cdot C \quad \text{kJ/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3:** Heat released by hydrogen combustion (to liquid water):
$$Q_H = 144200 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} = 1442 \cdot H \quad \text{kJ/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4:** Heat released by sulfur combustion:
$$Q_S = 9260 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} = 92.6 \cdot S \quad \text{kJ/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 (Heat Deductions):**

The oxygen already present in the fuel has pre-combusted some of the hydrogen (i.e., the H is partly already bound to O). The mass of hydrogen already combined with oxygen = $(O/8)$ kg H (since 8 kg O combines with 1 kg H to form water). This portion of H does NOT release heat.

So the **effective free hydrogen** = $\dfrac{H}{100} - \dfrac{O}{800}$ kg H per kg fuel.

The heat lost in vaporizing the moisture from H-combustion (and the inherent moisture M) is:
$$Q_{loss} = 2442 \cdot \left[9 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right] \text{ kJ/kg fuel}$$

(Here the factor 9 comes from the 8 kg O + 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O per kg H burnt.)

**Step 6: Net Higher Heating Value (HHV):**
$$HHV = 338.2\,C + 1442\,H + 92.6\,S - 2442 \cdot \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

**Step 7: Net Lower Heating Value (LHV):**
If we want LHV (where water leaves as vapor), subtract the latent heat of vaporization of all water produced (9H + M kg water per kg fuel):
$$LHV = HHV - 2442 \cdot \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

Substituting:
$$LHV = 338.2\,C + 1442\,H + 92.6\,S - 2 \cdot 2442 \cdot \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

Wait — let me correct this: the LHV formula is simply:
$$LHV = HHV - 2442 \cdot \dfrac{9H + M}{100}$$

So when we apply it to dry biomass (M = 0), and rearrange into the canonical Dulong form:

$$\boxed{\;CV = 0.3382\,C + 1.442\,H + 0.0926\,S - 0.0151\,M - 0.1249\,O\;\text{ MJ/kg}\;}$$

For low-moisture, low-ash biomass, a simplified empirical form often used in KTU problems is:

$$CV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A \quad \text{[MJ/kg]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small numerical differences between versions of Dulong's formula come from rounding of the bond-energy constants. The KTU 2024 board examiner will accept any of the standard textbook variants if the **physical interpretation** is correct.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A rice husk sample has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, by mass):
C = 38.5%, H = 4.7%, O = 35.8%, N = 0.6%, S = 0.1%, Ash = 20.3%. Moisture content = 8%. Calculate the **LHV** in MJ/kg.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the simplified Dulong formula for HHV (dry basis):
$$HHV = 0.3491 \cdot 38.5 + 1.1783 \cdot 4.7 + 0.1005 \cdot 0.1 - 0.1034 \cdot 35.8 - 0.0151 \cdot 0.6 - 0.0211 \cdot 20.3$$

Step 2 — Compute each term:
$$0.3491 \cdot 38.5 = 13.44 \text{ MJ/kg}$$
$$1.1783 \cdot 4.7 = 5.54 \text{ MJ/kg}$$
$$0.1005 \cdot 0.1 = 0.01 \text{ MJ/kg}$$
$$0.1034 \cdot 35.8 = 3.70 \text{ MJ/kg}$$
$$0.0151 \cdot 0.6 = 0.009 \text{ MJ/kg}$$
$$0.0211 \cdot 20.3 = 0.428 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

Step 3 — Sum the positive and negative terms:
$$HHV = (13.44 + 5.54 + 0.01) - (3.70 + 0.009 + 0.428)$$
$$HHV = 18.99 - 4.137 = 14.85 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

Step 4 — Convert to LHV by subtracting moisture latent heat (M = 8%):
$$LHV = HHV - 0.02442 \cdot (9H + M) \cdot 10$$
$$LHV = 14.85 - 0.02442 \cdot (9 \cdot 4.7 + 8) \cdot 10$$
$$LHV = 14.85 - 0.02442 \cdot (42.3 + 8) \cdot 10$$
$$LHV = 14.85 - 0.02442 \cdot 503$$
$$LHV = 14.85 - 12.28 \cdot \dfrac{1}{10}$$
$$LHV = 14.85 - 1.228 = 13.62 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> **Answer:** LHV of the rice husk = **13.62 MJ/kg** (matches the standard textbook value of 12–15 MJ/kg ✓)

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Released in Combustion of Biomass

For a system where $m_f$ kg of biomass is combusted with combustion efficiency $\eta_c$ in a boiler of efficiency $\eta_b$ feeding a Rankine cycle of efficiency $\eta_R$:

$$P_{elec} = m_f \cdot CV \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_b \cdot \eta_R \quad \text{[kW, with } m_f \text{ in kg/s]}$$

The specific CO₂ emission per unit electricity is:

$$CO_{2\,specific} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{P_{elec}} = \dfrac{3.67 \cdot \%C}{CV \cdot \eta_{overall}} \quad \text{[kg CO₂ / kWh]}$$

**Derivation of the 3.67 factor:**

The molar mass ratio: 1 mole of C (12 g) → 1 mole of CO₂ (44 g). So per kg of C burned, CO₂ produced = $44/12 = 3.67$ kg CO₂. Hence the carbon-to-CO₂ conversion factor is **3.67**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Biogas Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

The Buswell equation gives the theoretical maximum methane yield from biomass of known elemental composition $C_aH_bO_cN_d$:

$$C_aH_bO_cN_d + \left(\dfrac{4a - b - 2c + 3d}{4}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{4a + b - 2c - 3d}{8}\right) CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{4a - b + 2c + 3d}{8}\right) CO_2 + d \cdot NH_3$$

**Example:** For glucose, $C_6H_{12}O_6$ (no N), Buswell gives:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$

So 180 g of glucose yields 3 × 16 = 48 g of CH₄, i.e., **0.267 kg CH₄ per kg glucose**.
At STP, 1 mole of gas = 22.4 L, so 3 moles CH₄ = 67.2 L per 180 g = **0.373 m³ CH₄ per kg glucose**.

In real digesters, the actual yield is 60–80% of Buswell theoretical due to microbial maintenance energy and conversion losses.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conversion Technology Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly examinable** in KTU 2024. Students should be able to match a feedstock to a suitable conversion technology.

| Feedstock Type | Moisture | Best Conversion | Why? |
|----------------|----------|-----------------|------|
| Dry wood, pellets | <20% | **Direct combustion** | High energy density, low drying cost |
| Rice husk, sawdust | <20% | **Gasification** | Produces syngas for engines |
| Bagasse, straw | 10–25% | **Cogeneration** | Combined heat & power in sugar mills |
| Cow dung, manure | >80% | **Anaerobic digestion** | High moisture, wet digestion preferred |
| Sugarcane, corn | Wet | **Fermentation → ethanol** | Sugar content is directly fermentable |
| Vegetable oil | Liquid | **Transesterification → biodiesel** | Triglycerides react with methanol |
| Sewage sludge | >90% | **Anaerobic co-digestion** | Pathogen kill + energy recovery |
| Algae | >80% | **Hydrothermal liquefaction / lipid extraction** | High lipid content for biodiesel |
| MSW organic fraction | 50–70% | **Anaerobic digestion / incineration** | Diverts waste from landfill |
